```mermaid
    M2MNET -. "Upgraded with IP stack becomes" .-> IOTNET
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — M2M vs IoT Components

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph LAYERS["COMPARATIVE LAYERED MODEL"]
        L1["PERCEPTION LAYER\nSensors and RFID and Actuators"]
        L2["NETWORK LAYER\nTransport and Routing"]
        L3["PROCESSING LAYER\nCompute and Storage"]
        L4["APPLICATION LAYER\nServices and UI"]
    end

    subgraph M2MSTACK["M2M IMPLEMENTATION"]
        M1["Analog and Digital Sensors"]
        M2["Modbus and Zigbee and Cellular APN"]
        M3["Local SCADA Historian"]
        M4["Vendor Specific HMI"]
    end

    subgraph IOTSTACK["IoT IMPLEMENTATION"]
        I1["Smart Sensors with MCU"]
        I2["IP Stack MQTT and CoAP and HTTP"]
        I3["Edge Fog and Cloud Big Data"]
        I4["Web Mobile Voice AR VR"]
    end

    L1 --> M1
    L2 --> M2
    L3 --> M3
    L4 --> M4

    L1 --> I1
    L2 --> I2
    L3 --> I3
    L4 --> I4
```

## 4.3 Communication Pattern Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MPAT["M2M Pattern"]
        DEV1["Device A"]
        DEV2["Device B"]
        DEV1 -- "Proprietary Protocol" --> DEV2
    end

    subgraph IPAT["IoT Pattern"]
        ID1["Thing A"]
        ID2["Thing B"]
        ID3["Thing C"]
        ICLOUD["Cloud Server"]
        IUSER["User Smartphone"]
        ID1 -- "MQTT" --> ICLOUD
        ID2 -- "CoAP" --> ICLOUD
        ID3 -- "HTTP" --> ICLOUD
        ICLOUD -- "REST API" --> IUSER
        ID1 -- "D2D via Cloud" --> ID2
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The M2M pattern shows a **direct, locked-in** link between two devices. The IoT pattern shows a **hub-and-spoke cloud-mediated** model where multiple heterogeneous things converge on a shared cloud, which then serves end-users. The **D2D (Device-to-Device)** path in IoT goes *through* the cloud broker, unlike M2M where it is direct.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**Differentiate between M2M and IoT with respect to architecture style and communication protocols.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

* **Architecture Style (1.5 Marks):** M2M follows a **vertical, application-specific** architecture where a device communicates with a dedicated server of the same vendor (e.g., Modbus device ↔ Modbus SCADA). IoT follows a **horizontal, cross-domain** architecture where heterogeneous devices connect to shared cloud platforms using open internet protocols.
* **Communication Protocols (1.5 Marks):** M2M uses **proprietary, closed** protocols such as Modbus RTU, Profibus, CAN bus, and DNP3. IoT uses **open, IP-based** protocols such as MQTT, CoAP, HTTP/REST, and AMQP, allowing interoperability across vendors.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**List any three key features of IoT that distinguish it from traditional M2M systems.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Internet-based Open Standards:** IoT uses universally accepted IP-based protocols (TCP/IP, HTTP, MQTT), unlike M2M's vendor-locked protocols. (1 Mark)
2. **Cloud and Big Data Integration:** IoT leverages scalable cloud platforms for storage, analytics, and ML, while M2M relies on local SCADA servers. (1 Mark)
3. **Human-to-Machine (H2M) Interaction:** IoT allows end-users to interact with devices via smartphones and dashboards; M2M is fully autonomous with no human in the loop. (1 Mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) carrying **7 marks** and (b) carrying **7 marks**.

---

### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks] Explain the layered architecture of M2M communication. Describe the function of each layer with suitable examples.**

**Model Solution:**

The M2M architecture typically consists of **five functional layers** as standardized by ETSI (European Telecommunications Standards Institute):

1. **Device/Gateway Layer (1 Mark):** Contains the M2M device hardware — sensors, actuators, RFID tags, and the M2M gateway (e.g., a remote terminal unit RTU in a substation). It performs physical signal acquisition and actuation.

2. **Network/Communication Layer (1 Mark):** Provides connectivity between the device and the M2M server. Uses cellular (2G/3G/LTE-M), Zigbee, Wi-Fi, satellite, or fixed-line (DSL). Example: A fleet management system uses **GPRS** to transmit GPS coordinates from trucks.

3. **M2M Service/Platform Layer (1 Mark):** Acts as middleware that manages device registration, authentication, data aggregation, and routing. Example: The SCADA master station in an oil refinery receives hundreds of sensor values and time-stamps them.

4. **Application Layer (1 Mark):** Hosts the business logic and domain-specific applications — leak detection in pipelines, predictive maintenance of turbines, automated meter reading (AMR).

5. **Business/Management Layer (1 Mark):** Provides analytics, billing, reporting, and decision-support. Example: A utility company uses aggregated M2M data to forecast load demand and plan infrastructure.

* **Block Diagram (1 Mark):** *(Students should draw a 5-layer stack diagram — Device → Network → Platform → Application → Business.)*
* **Suitable Real-World Example (1 Mark):** *Automatic Meter Reading (AMR) system* — the smart meter (device) sends consumption data over cellular (network) to a metering server (platform), which feeds a billing engine (application), enabling the utility to generate monthly bills (business layer).

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Compare and contrast M2M and IoT across 7 critical parameters. Justify why IoT is considered an evolution of M2M.**

**Model Solution:**

| **S.No.** | **Parameter** | **M2M** | **IoT** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Architecture | Vertical, single vendor | Horizontal, multi-vendor |
| 2 | Protocols | Proprietary (Modbus, CAN) | Open (MQTT, CoAP, HTTP) |
| 3 | Communication Scope | Device ↔ Device / Device ↔ Server | Device ↔ Cloud + H2M |
| 4 | Data Volume | Low (KB/day) | Massive (TB/day) |
| 5 | Intelligence | Centralized (SCADA) | Distributed (Edge + Cloud) |
| 6 | Interoperability | Vendor-locked | Open standards / Semantic |
| 7 | Scalability | Limited (10²–10⁴ devices) | Massive (10⁹+ devices via IPv6) |

* **Justification — Why IoT is an Evolution of M2M (1 Mark for synthesis):** IoT evolved from M2M by adding (i) **internet-based global connectivity** (IPv6 addressing of 2¹²⁸), (ii) **cloud and edge computing** for scalable intelligence, (iii) **H2M interfaces** that involve human decision-making, and (iv) **semantic interoperability** allowing cross-industry data sharing. M2M is the **technological foundation**; IoT is the **internet-enabled, intelligence-augmented, user-centric** superset.
* **Award marks for:** Clarity of comparison (3 Marks), Parameter coverage (3 Marks), Justification conclusion (1 Mark).

---

### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Alternate Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks] With a neat block diagram, explain the 5-layer IoT reference architecture. List the protocols used in each layer.**

**Model Solution:**

The standard 5-layer IoT reference model is:

1. **Perception/Sensing Layer (1.5 Marks):** The physical layer where sensors (temperature, humidity, motion, gas) and actuators gather environmental data. **Protocols/Hardware:** Zigbee, BLE, RFID, 6LoWPAN, IEEE 802.15.4.

2. **Network/Transport Layer (1.5 Marks):** Transmits collected data to the processing center. **Protocols:** Wi-Fi, Ethernet, 4G/5G, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT. At the application transport level, **MQTT, CoAP, AMQP** are used.

3. **Middleware/Processing Layer (1 Mark):** Performs data storage, filtering, aggregation, and analysis. Implemented via **edge gateways, fog nodes, and cloud platforms** (AWS IoT Core, Azure IoT Hub).

4. **Application Layer (1 Mark):** Provides domain-specific services such as smart home automation, industrial control, health monitoring. Delivered via mobile apps, web dashboards, or voice assistants (Alexa, Google Assistant).

5. **Business Layer (1 Mark):** Manages the entire IoT system — user privacy, revenue models, application lifecycle, regulatory compliance. Uses tools like **blockchain for trust** and **AI/ML for predictive business analytics**.

* **Block Diagram (1 Mark):** *(Draw a 5-tier vertical stack with the layers labelled top-to-bottom as Business → Application → Processing → Network → Perception, with arrows showing bi-directional data flow.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Discuss 4 real-world applications each of M2M and IoT. Explain why a particular use-case cannot be migrated from M2M to IoT easily.**

**Model Solution:**

**M2M Applications (2 Marks — list with 0.5 each):**

1. **SCADA-based Power Grid Monitoring** — Real-time telemetry of transformers.
2. **Industrial Robotic Arms in Assembly Lines** — Proprietary CAN bus coordination.
3. **Automatic Teller Machines (ATMs)** — Encrypted leased-line transactions.
4. **Vending Machine Stock Alerts** — Cellular M2M with private APN.

**IoT Applications (2 Marks — list with 0.5 each):**

1. **Smart Home Automation** — Alexa + smart lights + smart locks.
2. **Wearable Health Monitoring** — Smartwatch sending ECG to cloud.
3. **Precision Agriculture** — Soil sensors + drone imagery + AI.
4. **Smart City Traffic Management** — Adaptive signals + connected vehicles.

**Why Some Use-Cases Resist M2M-to-IoT Migration (3 Marks):**

* **Latency-Critical Industrial Control (e.g., Robotic Arm):** Migrating from CAN bus to IP-based IoT introduces **unpredictable TCP/IP stack latency** (jitter of 1–10 ms over public internet), violating the **deterministic sub-millisecond** response time required for synchronized robotic motion.
* **Air-Gapped Security (e.g., Nuclear SCADA):** Connecting a reactor monitoring system to the public internet (a requirement of IoT) violates the **NERC-CIP and IEC 62645** security mandates. M2M's air-gapped private network is mandated by regulation, not by preference.
* **High Cost of Retrofitting:** Legacy industrial devices use **8-bit microcontrollers without TCP/IP stacks**, making firmware overhaul and certification prohibitively expensive — often 5–10× the original hardware cost.
* **Data Sovereignty:** Industries like defense and healthcare must keep data on-premise due to GDPR/HIPAA, conflicting with IoT's default cloud-centric model.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Writing "M2M and IoT are the same"** — *ZERO marks* for this. M2M ⊂ IoT, they are not synonymous. KTU examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Confusing "horizontal" and "vertical" architecture** — Students often swap these terms. **Vertical = M2M** (one industry, one vendor). **Horizontal = IoT** (cross-industry, multi-vendor).
> 3. **Forgetting to name protocols** — Generic statements like "M2M uses different protocols" earn partial credit. You **must** name at least **2 specific protocols** for full marks (e.g., Modbus + Zigbee for M2M; MQTT + CoAP for IoT).
> 4. **Skipping the diagram** — For 7-mark sub-parts, a **block diagram is mandatory**. A neat 5-layer architecture diagram typically earns **1–2 marks extra** as visual support.
> 5. **Ignoring H2M interaction** — Many students forget that IoT adds **Human-to-Machine** communication as a *third* type. This is a frequently asked KTU 2024 Scheme question.
> 6. **Not stating that IoT is a superset of M2M** — Use the phrase **"IoT is a superset of M2M"** explicitly in your answer. It is a high-weightage KTU keyword.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

* **M2M** = **M**achine-to-**M**achine, point-to-point, vertical, proprietary, SCADA-based, no humans in the loop.
* **IoT** = **I**nternet **o**f **T**hings, cloud-based, horizontal, open standards, H2M + M2M + D2D, intelligent.
* **Set Relation:** $M2M \subset IoT$ (M2M is a *proper subset* of IoT). Always write this in the exam.
* **Key Architectural Difference:** M2M is **vertical silo**; IoT is **horizontal ecosystem**.
* **Protocol Comparison:** M2M → Modbus, Profibus, CAN, DNP3 (proprietary). IoT → MQTT, CoAP, HTTP, AMQP, LWM2M (open, IP-based).
* **Addressing:** M2M uses vendor IDs; IoT uses **IPv6 (2¹²⁸ addresses)**.
* **Data Flow:** M2M → Device → Local SCADA. IoT → Device → Edge → Cloud → User.
* **Communication Types in IoT:** Device-to-Device, Device-to-Gateway, Device-to-Cloud, **Human-to-Machine**.
* **Examples to Memorize:** M2M = ATMs, vending machines, SCADA. IoT = Smart home, wearables, smart cities, precision agriculture.
* **ETSI M2M Layers:** Device, Network, Platform, Application, Business (5 layers).
* **IoT Reference Model Layers:** Perception, Network, Middleware/Processing, Application, Business (5 layers).
* **Always draw a comparison table** in 14-mark answers — it is the highest-scoring format.
* **One-line takeaway:** *M2M is the "machine talking to machine"; IoT is the "machine talking to the world and the world talking back".*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Typical Application Areas of IoT - Smart Homes, Agriculture and Smart City
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET721 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Typical Application Areas of IoT - Smart Homes, Agriculture and Smart City

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Typical Application Areas" of IoT?

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEAET721 – Internet of Things)**, the phrase *"Typical Application Areas of IoT"* refers to the **canonical, real-world vertical domains** where the IoT paradigm — the network of physical objects embedded with sensors, software, and connectivity — is deployed to automate monitoring, control, and decision-making. The three flagship verticals mandated by the syllabus are:

1. **Smart Homes** (Consumer / Home Automation)
2. **Smart Agriculture** (Precision Farming / AgriTech)
3. **Smart Cities** (Urban Infrastructure Management)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The 2024 scheme expects students to not merely *list* these areas, but to **map each vertical to its sensing, communication, and actuation layers**, identify the KPIs (Key Performance Indicators) used to measure "smartness," and recognize the **cross-cutting challenges** (interoperability, security, energy).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> An *IoT Application Area* is a domain-specific ecosystem in which constrained or unconstrained devices, interconnected via IP or non-IP protocols, generate data that is processed (locally or in the cloud) to deliver an automated, context-aware service to an end-user or stakeholder.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Nervous System" View

Think of the **Internet of Things as the digital nervous system of the physical world**.

- **Sensors** = *sensory receptors* (skin, eyes) — they detect temperature, motion, light, soil moisture.
- **Connectivity** (Wi-Fi, LoRa, ZigBee, NB-IoT) = *nerves* — they carry signals.
- **Cloud / Edge Processing** = *the brain* — it interprets signals and makes decisions.
- **Actuators** (smart locks, irrigation valves, traffic signals) = *muscles* — they execute the decision.

### Real-world Analogy

Imagine a **traditional Kerala "Nalukettu" house** (an analogy students relate to):
- In a *non-smart* home, the family must manually open windows when it gets hot, switch on the pump when the well runs low, and check the door latch at night.
- In a *Smart Home*, tiny digital "helpers" (sensors) continuously watch the environment, send the readings to a "brain" (the home gateway or cloud), and the brain commands "muscles" (actuators) to open a window, start the pump, or lock the door — **all without human intervention**.

The same principle scales up: from one house → one farm → an entire city.

---

## 1.3 Core Building Blocks Common to ALL Three Areas

| Layer | Purpose | Typical Components |
|---|---|---|
| **Perception Layer** | Sense the physical world | Temperature, humidity, PIR, soil-moisture, gas, vibration, flow sensors |
| **Network Layer** | Transport data | Wi-Fi, BLE, ZigBee, LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, 4G/5G |
| **Processing Layer** | Analyze and decide | Edge gateways, fog nodes, cloud servers, ML models |
| **Application Layer** | Deliver the service | Mobile apps, dashboards, voice assistants, SMS alerts |

> [!NOTE]
> **Metric to remember:** The "smartness" of any IoT deployment is judged on **4 KPIs**:
> 1. **Latency** (ms)
> 2. **Energy Efficiency** (Joules / battery life)
> 3. **Scalability** (number of nodes supported)
> 4. **Reliability / Uptime** (%)

---

## 1.4 Why These Three Areas? The KTU Reasoning

The KTU syllabus selects **Smart Homes, Agriculture, and Cities** because they collectively cover the **three scales of IoT deployment**:

- **Personal scale** (Smart Home) — Body-Area / Personal-Area Networks
- **Outdoor / Environmental scale** (Smart Agriculture) — Wide-Area Networks, LPWAN
- **Urban / Civic scale** (Smart City) — Metropolitan / Mass-scale deployment

> [!TIP]
> **Board Exam Tip:** When asked "Why are these three considered typical?", answer along the lines of *scale diversity* and *socio-economic impact* — KTU examiners reward this mapping.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

This section dissects each of the three application areas — **what is sensed, how it is networked, how it is processed, and what value it generates.**

---

## 2.1 Smart Home (Home Automation)

### 2.1.1 Definition
A **Smart Home** is a residence equipped with a network of interconnected sensors, actuators, and controllers that enable **remote monitoring, automation, and optimization** of household functions such as lighting, HVAC (Heating, Ventilation, Air Conditioning), security, and appliance control.

### 2.1.2 Operational Breakdown
- **Perception:** Door/window reed switches, PIR motion sensors, temperature/humidity DHT22, smoke (MQ-2), gas (MQ-5), smart energy meters, water-flow sensors.
- **Communication:** Primarily **short-range, indoor protocols**:
  - Wi-Fi (high bandwidth, e.g., smart cameras)
  - ZigBee / Z-Wave (low power mesh, e.g., smart bulbs)
  - BLE (low energy, e.g., wearables talking to hub)
- **Processing:** Local **Home Gateway** (e.g., Raspberry Pi running Home Assistant) + optional cloud (AWS IoT, Google Home).
- **Actuation:** Smart switches, smart locks, motorized curtain rods, smart geysers, voice assistants (Alexa / Google Assistant).
- **User Interface:** Mobile app, voice, or wall-mounted touch panel.

### 2.1.3 High-Value Use Cases
1. **Intrusion Detection:** PIR + door sensor → push notification + auto-lock.
2. **Energy Management:** Smart meter → schedule high-load appliances during off-peak tariff hours.
3. **Fire / Gas Safety:** Smoke sensor → cut off gas valve, send SMS, trigger alarm.
4. **Elderly / Child Care:** Fall detection, presence detection, automatic light following.

### 2.1.4 Key Engineering Trade-off
| Parameter | ZigBee | Wi-Fi | BLE |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Range** | 10–100 m | 30–50 m | 10 m |
| **Power** | Very low | High | Very low |
| **Data Rate** | 250 kbps | >100 Mbps | 2 Mbps |
| **Topology** | Mesh | Star | Star / P2P |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Smart home devices form a **WPAN (Wireless Personal Area Network)** — the smallest geographic IoT footprint, but with the **highest device density per unit area**.

---

## 2.2 Smart Agriculture (Precision Farming / Smart Farming)

### 2.2.1 Definition
**Smart Agriculture** uses IoT to monitor micro-climatic and soil conditions in real time, enabling **precision irrigation, fertilization, and pest control** that maximize yield while minimizing water, fertilizer, and pesticide consumption.

### 2.2.2 Operational Breakdown
- **Perception (in-field):**
  - Soil moisture (capacitive sensors, e.g., VH-400)
  - Soil pH and NPK (Nitrogen-Phosphorus-Potassium) sensors
  - Air temperature, humidity, rainfall
  - Leaf wetness, solar radiation (pyranometer)
  - Drones with multispectral cameras for crop health (NDVI index)
- **Communication:** Mostly **LPWAN** because farms span hectares:
  - **LoRaWAN** (long range, multi-km, low data rate)
  - **NB-IoT** (cellular, operator-managed)
  - **Sigfox** (ultra-narrowband)
  - Local **ZigBee** mesh for in-greenhouse networks
- **Processing:** Edge gateway (e.g., LoRa + Raspberry Pi) + cloud dashboard (e.g., ThingSpeak, AWS IoT, custom).
- **Actuation:** Solenoid valves for drip irrigation, motor starters for pumps, drones for spraying.

### 2.2.3 Why LPWAN?
Agricultural fields are **large, rural, often off-grid**. The chosen protocol must satisfy:
- **Range:** > 2 km
- **Battery life:** > 5 years (no mains power in field)
- **Data rate:** low (a moisture reading every 15 min is sufficient)

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of thumb taught in KTU labs:**
> $$ \text{Battery Life (years)} \approx \frac{\text{Battery Capacity (mAh)} \times \text{Voltage}}{\text{Average Current Draw (mA)} \times 8760 \text{ hours/year}} $$

### 2.2.4 High-Value Use Cases
1. **Precision Irrigation:** Soil moisture < threshold → open valve for X minutes.
2. **Greenhouse Automation:** Temperature > 35 °C → open roof vents, start foggers.
3. **Livestock Monitoring:** Wearable collar with GPS + heart-rate → detect sickness early.
4. **Pest / Disease Prediction:** Weather + leaf-wetness data → ML model predicts blight risk.

---

## 2.3 Smart City (Urban IoT)

### 2.3.1 Definition
A **Smart City** integrates IoT across **urban infrastructure** — transportation, utilities, public safety, waste management, environment, and citizen services — to improve **quality of life, sustainability, and operational efficiency** of the city administration.

### 2.3.2 Operational Breakdown
| Sub-vertical | Sensors | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| **Smart Parking** | Ultrasonic / magnetic sensors in each bay | Real-time parking availability → reduced traffic congestion |
| **Smart Streetlight** | Ambient light + PIR | Dim/brighten based on pedestrian presence → ~40–60 % energy savings |
| **Smart Waste** | Ultrasonic fill-level sensor in bin | Route optimization for garbage trucks |
| **Smart Water** | Flow, pressure, quality (pH, chlorine) sensors | Leak detection, quality monitoring |
| **Smart Air Quality** | PM2.5, PM10, NO₂, CO, O₃ | Pollution heatmaps, public alerts |
| **Smart Traffic** | Inductive loops, cameras, ANPR | Adaptive signal timing, congestion pricing |
| **Smart Surveillance** | CCTV + AI analytics | Crowd density, accident detection |
| **Smart Grid** | Smart meters, transformer monitors | Dynamic load balancing, outage detection |

### 2.3.3 Communication Backbone
Smart cities use a **heterogeneous network**:
- **Fiber + 5G** for high-bandwidth backhaul (CCTV, traffic cameras)
- **LPWAN (LoRa/NB-IoT)** for utility meters and bins
- **Wi-Fi hotspots** for public access
- **RSUs (Roadside Units)** with DSRC for V2X

### 2.3.4 KPI Examples
- **Bin fill level alerts:** reduce collection trips by ~30 %.
- **Smart streetlights:** reduce municipal energy bill by up to **50 %**.
- **Adaptive traffic signals:** reduce average commute time by **15–25 %**.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every quantitative formula/relationship that may be asked in an exam for this topic. **Memorize the units.**

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Battery life (IoT node) | $T = \dfrac{C \cdot V}{I_{\text{avg}} \cdot 8760}$ | $C$ = capacity (mAh), $V$ = voltage (V), $I_{\text{avg}}$ = average current (mA), $T$ = life in years |
| 2 | Link budget (wireless) | $P_{\text{RX}} = P_{\text{TX}} + G_{\text{TX}} + G_{\text{RX}} - L_{\text{path}}$ | All in dB / dBm |
| 3 | Free-space path loss (FSPL) | $\text{FSPL (dB)} = 20 \log_{10}(d) + 20 \log_{10}(f) + 32.44$ | $d$ in km, $f$ in MHz |
| 4 | Sampling interval vs battery | $I_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{I_{\text{active}} \cdot t_{\text{tx}} + I_{\text{sleep}} \cdot (T_{\text{period}} - t_{\text{tx}})}{T_{\text{period}}}$ | Currents in mA, times in s |
| 5 | Coverage of LoRaWAN (approx) | $R \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{P_{\text{TX}} \cdot G \cdot \lambda^{2}}{(4\pi)^{2} \cdot P_{\text{min}}}}$ | $R$ = range (m), $\lambda$ = wavelength (m) |
| 6 | Number of packets per day | $N_{p} = \dfrac{86400}{T_{\text{period}} \text{ (s)}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Air Quality Index (simplified) | $\text{AQI} = \dfrac{I_{\text{high}} - I_{\text{low}}}{C_{\text{high}} - C_{\text{low}}} (C - C_{\text{low}}) + I_{\text{low}}$ | Linear breakpoint interpolation |
| 8 | Smart light energy saving | $\%\text{Saving} = 1 - \dfrac{E_{\text{dyn}}}{E_{\text{const}}}$ | $E_{\text{dyn}}$ = dynamic energy, $E_{\text{const}}$ = always-on energy |
| 9 | Data size per sample | $S = n \cdot s \cdot 8$ bits | $n$ = number of sensors, $s$ = bytes per sample |
| 10 | Smart home device density | $\rho = \dfrac{N_{\text{devices}}}{A_{\text{home}}} \text{ (devices/m}^{2}\text{)}$ | $N$ = device count, $A$ = home area |

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Exam Pitfall:** Never write a table cell containing a vertical bar `|`. KTU digital valuation systems may break the markdown / LaTeX rendering. In your answer sheet, use `\vert` or `\mid`.

---

## 2.5 Real-world Utility Across Engineering Fields

| IoT Vertical | Cross-disciplinary Engineering Utility |
|---|---|
| Smart Home | Embedded systems, mobile computing, HCI, renewable energy integration |
| Smart Agriculture | Agronomy + environmental science, fluid mechanics (drip), remote sensing, GIS |
| Smart City | Civil (urban planning), electrical (smart grid), environmental (air quality), CS (data analytics) |

This **multi-disciplinary nature** is precisely why KTU places these in the Open Elective — they showcase the breadth of IoT.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs

This section provides **worked analytical examples** that a KTU student can use to answer Part B numerical / analytical sub-questions. Every step is shown explicitly.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Battery Life of a Soil-Moisture Sensor (Smart Agriculture)

### Problem Statement
A LoRaWAN soil-moisture sensor in a smart farm transmits **once every 30 minutes**. Each transmission lasts **1.2 seconds** and consumes **120 mA** at **3.6 V**. In sleep mode, the node draws **8 µA**. The battery is a **2,400 mAh, 3.6 V** Li-SOCl₂ cell. Estimate the **battery life in years**.

### Step 1: Identify the duty cycle
The node is in *active* (TX) mode for 1.2 s out of every 1,800 s (30 min).

$$
\text{Duty Cycle} = \frac{1.2}{1800} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4}
$$

### Step 2: Compute the average current draw

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = (I_{\text{tx}} \cdot \text{Duty}) + (I_{\text{sleep}} \cdot (1 - \text{Duty}))
$$

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = (120 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-4}) + (0.008 \times (1 - 6.667 \times 10^{-4}))
$$

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = 0.0800 \; \text{mA} + 0.00799 \; \text{mA} \approx 0.0880 \; \text{mA}
$$

### Step 3: Compute battery life in hours

$$
T_{\text{hours}} = \frac{\text{Capacity (mAh)}}{I_{\text{avg}} \text{ (mA)}} = \frac{2400}{0.0880}
$$

$$
T_{\text{hours}} \approx 27{,}272 \text{ hours}
$$

### Step 4: Convert to years

$$
T_{\text{years}} = \frac{27{,}272}{8760} \approx 3.11 \text{ years}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** Battery life ≈ **3.1 years**, which is *below* the typical 5–10 year LPWAN design target. To improve it, a KTU student should suggest: reduce TX frequency to once per 2 hours, use a higher-capacity battery (e.g., 6,500 mAh), or use an energy-harvesting solar panel.

### Step 5: Re-design (Optional — common Part B follow-up)

If we use the same battery but transmit **once every 2 hours**:

$$
T_{\text{period}} = 7200 \text{ s}, \quad \text{Duty} = \frac{1.2}{7200} = 1.667 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = (120 \times 1.667 \times 10^{-4}) + (0.008 \times 0.99983) \approx 0.0280 \text{ mA}
$$

$$
T_{\text{years}} = \frac{2400}{0.0280 \times 8760} \approx 9.78 \text{ years}
$$

This satisfies the LPWAN design target.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Smart Streetlight Energy Saving (Smart City)

### Problem Statement
A city has **10,000** conventional streetlights, each rated **150 W**, operating **12 hours every night**. The municipality replaces them with smart LED streetlights rated **60 W** with PIR dimming (drops to **20 W** when no one is nearby; 70 % of the night is "no movement"). The cost of electricity is **₹7 per kWh**. Calculate the **annual energy saved (kWh) and annual money saved (₹)**.

### Step 1: Conventional energy per night (per lamp)

$$
E_{\text{conv}} = P \times t = 150 \text{ W} \times 12 \text{ h} = 1.8 \text{ kWh/night}
$$

### Step 2: Smart energy per night (per lamp)
With 30 % of time at full 60 W and 70 % at 20 W:

$$
E_{\text{smart}} = (60 \times 0.30 \times 12) + (20 \times 0.70 \times 12)
$$

$$
E_{\text{smart}} = 216 + 168 = 384 \text{ Wh/night} = 0.384 \text{ kWh/night}
$$

### Step 3: Savings per lamp per night

$$
\Delta E_{\text{lamp}} = 1.8 - 0.384 = 1.416 \text{ kWh/night}
$$

### Step 4: Total savings for 10,000 lamps (annual)

$$
\Delta E_{\text{total}} = 1.416 \times 10{,}000 \times 365
$$

$$
\Delta E_{\text{total}} = 5{,}168{,}400 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

### Step 5: Money saved

$$
\text{Savings} = 5{,}168{,}400 \times 7 = ₹ 36{,}178{,}800 \approx ₹ 3.62 \text{ crore/year}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Even though the smart streetlight hardware costs more upfront, payback periods for Indian municipal projects typically range **3–5 years** purely from energy savings.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Smart Home Data-Load Estimation

### Problem Statement
A smart home has **8 IoT devices**, each producing a **12-byte** telemetry packet **every 10 seconds**. Estimate the **daily data volume (MB)** generated and decide whether **Wi-Fi** or **BLE** is appropriate for backhaul.

### Step 1: Packets per device per day

$$
N_p = \frac{86400}{10} = 8640 \text{ packets/device/day}
$$

### Step 2: Bytes per device per day

$$
S_{\text{device}} = 8640 \times 12 = 103{,}680 \text{ bytes/day} \approx 101.25 \text{ KB/day}
$$

### Step 3: Total home data per day

$$
S_{\text{total}} = 8 \times 103{,}680 = 829{,}440 \text{ bytes/day} \approx 0.79 \text{ MB/day}
$$

### Step 4: Required data rate

$$
R = \frac{0.79 \text{ MB} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte}}{86400 \text{ s}} \approx 73 \text{ bits/s per home}
$$

### Step 5: Choice of protocol
- **BLE** (max 2 Mbps) — technically supports 73 bps, but its **star topology and 10 m range** make it impractical for whole-home coverage.
- **Wi-Fi** (typical 50+ Mbps, full-home coverage) — clearly the better choice for a *gateway uplink*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Per-device short-haul:** ZigBee or BLE remains the right choice between sensor and hub; Wi-Fi is for hub-to-cloud.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Application Areas

| Dimension | Smart Home | Smart Agriculture | Smart City |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Geographic scale** | 1 home (10² m²) | 1 farm (10³–10⁵ m²) | 1 city (10⁷–10⁹ m²) |
| **Power source** | Mains + battery | Mostly battery / solar | Mains (with backup) |
| **Primary protocol** | Wi-Fi, ZigBee, BLE | LoRaWAN, NB-IoT, ZigBee | 5G, Fiber, LoRa, Wi-Fi |
| **Data rate need** | Medium (cameras) | Low (sensors) | Mixed (high + low) |
| **Latency requirement** | Medium (1 s OK) | High tolerance (min) | Low (ms for traffic) |
| **Biggest challenge** | Privacy, interoperability | Rural connectivity, cost | Citizen data privacy, scale |
| **Key KPI** | Comfort + energy saved | Yield / litre-of-water | Commute time, AQI, downtime |
| **Stakeholder** | Homeowner | Farmer | Municipal corporation |
| **Device density** | Very high | Low–medium | Very high |
| **Data storage** | Local + cloud | Cloud-heavy | Edge + cloud + data lake |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic (KTU trick):** **HAC** — **H**ome, **A**griculture, **C**ity. Remember it as *"Home, Agri, City = HAC… like hack the planet."*
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> All questions follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEAET721)** pattern: descriptive, application-oriented, and mapped to **CO1 / CO2** (per the 2024 syllabus). Bloom levels span **Remember, Understand, Apply, Analyze**.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 marks = 6 marks)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Expected)]** *(3 Marks, Bloom: Understand)*
**List and briefly explain the four layers of a generic IoT architecture with an example component from a Smart Home system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Perception Layer** — interacts with the physical world using sensors (e.g., DHT22 for temperature/humidity). *(0.75 m)*
2. **Network Layer** — transmits data using protocols like Wi-Fi, ZigBee, BLE (e.g., ZigBee mesh for smart bulbs). *(0.75 m)*
3. **Processing Layer** — analyses and stores data; can be edge (Raspberry Pi) or cloud (AWS IoT). *(0.75 m)*
4. **Application Layer** — delivers the service to the user via mobile app, dashboard or voice (e.g., Google Home app). *(0.75 m)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Expected)]** *(3 Marks, Bloom: Remember)*
**Differentiate between Smart Home and Smart City IoT deployments in terms of (a) geographic scale, (b) primary communication protocol, and (c) biggest challenge.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Smart Home | Smart City |
|---|---|---|
| **(a) Geographic scale** | A single residence (~10² m²) | An entire urban area (~10⁷–10⁹ m²) |
| **(b) Primary protocol** | Wi-Fi / ZigBee / BLE | 5G / Fiber / LoRaWAN |
| **(c) Biggest challenge** | Interoperability and user privacy | Scale, data privacy and real-time processing |

*(1 mark per row)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Question Choice (Choose ANY ONE — 14 Marks)

### Question A — 14 Marks *(Bloom: Apply + Analyze)*

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Expected) – ESE Module 1 Internal Choice Option 1]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a Smart Agriculture system**. Identify the sensors used and justify the choice of LoRaWAN as the communication protocol. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A LoRaWAN soil-moisture sensor operates at **3.6 V**, with a battery capacity of **2,400 mAh**. Active TX current is **120 mA** for **1.2 s**, and sleep current is **8 µA**. If the sensor wakes up **once every 15 minutes**, calculate the **average current draw** and the **battery life in years**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part A (a) — 7 marks

1. **Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the architecture with **Perception** (soil moisture, DHT22, pH sensor, leaf wetness, drone), **Network** (LoRaWAN gateway, NB-IoT), **Processing** (Edge Pi + AWS IoT), **Application** (Farmer mobile app + SMS), and **Actuation** (solenoid valve, motor). *Refer to Section 4.3.*

2. **Sensors (2 marks):**
   - Soil moisture: VH-400 capacitive
   - Weather: DHT22, rain gauge, pyranometer
   - Soil chemistry: pH and NPK sensors
   - Crop health: drone with multispectral camera (NDVI)

3. **Justification for LoRaWAN (2 marks):**
   - Range up to **10–15 km** in rural line-of-sight — covers a large farm with one gateway.
   - Battery life **> 5 years** because of low duty cycle and ~10–14 dBm TX power.
   - Penetration through vegetation is acceptable at sub-GHz bands (868/915 MHz).
   - Data rate is low (0.3–50 kbps) — sufficient for periodic sensor telemetry.
   - **Alternative considered:** Wi-Fi — rejected due to limited range and high power; ZigBee — rejected for >100 m range.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Period conversion (0.5 mark):**

$$
T_{\text{period}} = 15 \text{ min} = 15 \times 60 = 900 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Duty cycle (1 mark):**

$$
\text{Duty} = \frac{1.2}{900} = 1.333 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 3 — Average current (2 marks):**

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = (120 \times 1.333 \times 10^{-3}) + (0.008 \times (1 - 1.333 \times 10^{-3}))
$$

$$
I_{\text{avg}} = 0.160 + 0.00799 \approx 0.168 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 4 — Battery life in hours (1 mark):**

$$
T_{\text{h}} = \frac{2400}{0.168} = 14{,}285.7 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to years (1 mark):**

$$
T_{\text{y}} = \frac{14{,}285.7}{8760} \approx 1.63 \text{ years}
$$

**Step 6 — Design commentary (1.5 marks):**
- 1.6 years is **below** the typical 5-year LPWAN target.
- Recommendations: increase TX interval to **once per 2 h** (boosts to ~9.7 years), use a **larger battery (6,500 mAh)** which alone doubles life to ~3.2 years, or add a **solar harvester** to extend it indefinitely.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks *(Bloom: Understand + Apply)*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Expected) – ESE Module 1 Internal Choice Option 2]**

**(a)** Describe the **Smart Home application area** of IoT. List at least **6 different IoT devices** found in a typical smart home and explain the role of the **home gateway**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A city corporation plans to install **8,000 smart streetlights** to replace the existing 150 W HPSV lamps. The new LED lamps are **60 W**, with adaptive dimming to **20 W** when no movement is detected. The lights are on for **12 hours/night**. Assume **70 %** of the night has no movement. The cost of electricity is **₹8 per kWh**. Calculate: *(i)* the **daily energy saved per lamp**, *(ii)* the **total annual energy saved (kWh)** for the city, and *(iii)* the **annual money saved (₹)**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part B (a) — 7 marks

1. **Definition (1.5 marks):** A Smart Home uses IoT to enable remote monitoring, automation and optimization of household functions such as lighting, HVAC, security and appliances.

2. **Six smart-home devices (3 marks — 0.5 each):**
   1. Smart bulb (Philips Hue / ZigBee)
   2. Smart door lock (August / Yale)
   3. PIR motion sensor (Aqara)
   4. Smart thermostat (Nest)
   5. Smart energy plug (TP-Link Kasa)
   6. Smoke + gas alarm (MQ-2 + smart buzzer)
   7. (Bonus) Smart curtain motor / voice assistant

3. **Role of the home gateway (2.5 marks):**
   - Acts as a **bridge** between short-range devices (ZigBee/BLE) and the IP network (Wi-Fi/Ethernet).
   - Runs a **local rules engine** (e.g., "if motion AND dark → turn on light") for low-latency response even when internet is down.
   - Provides **security** by isolating devices on a separate VLAN or subnet.
   - Performs **protocol translation** (e.g., ZigBee → MQTT → cloud).
   - Acts as a **data aggregator** before sending summaries to the cloud, reducing bandwidth.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B (b) — 7 marks

**(i) Daily energy saved per lamp — 2 marks:**

$$
E_{\text{conv}} = 150 \text{ W} \times 12 \text{ h} = 1.8 \text{ kWh/night}
$$

$$
E_{\text{smart}} = (60 \times 0.30 \times 12) + (20 \times 0.70 \times 12) = 216 + 168 = 384 \text{ Wh} = 0.384 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
\Delta E_{\text{lamp}} = 1.8 - 0.384 = 1.416 \text{ kWh/night}
$$

**(ii) Annual energy saved — 2.5 marks:**

$$
\Delta E_{\text{total}} = 1.416 \times 8000 \times 365
$$

$$
\Delta E_{\text{total}} = 4{,}134{,}720 \text{ kWh/year} \approx 4.13 \text{ million kWh/year}
$$

**(iii) Annual money saved — 2.5 marks:**

$$
\text{Savings} = 4{,}134{,}720 \times 8 = ₹ 33{,}077{,}760 \approx ₹ 3.31 \text{ crore/year}
$$

**Bonus commentary (optional 0.5):** Assuming a smart streetlight costs ~₹15,000 installed vs. ~₹5,000 for HPSV, the **incremental capex** is ~₹80 crore — payback ≈ 80 / 3.31 ≈ **24 years** at ₹8/kWh. To improve payback, the city should **negotiate EPC-mode bidding** (CapEx paid from savings).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common ways KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Listing without explaining** — writing "Smart Home, Agriculture, City" without mapping each to its **sensors, protocol, and one KPI** loses 60 % of credit. Always use a structured table or layered diagram.
> 2. **Confusing protocols** — Wi-Fi is *not* typically used as the in-field sensor protocol in smart agriculture; it is **LoRaWAN / NB-IoT / ZigBee**. Markers specifically deduct for this.
> 3. **Skipping the "Smart" qualifier** — calling it "Agriculture" instead of "Smart Agriculture / Precision Farming" loses a mark.
> 4. **Numerical errors in battery-life problems** — using **8 mA instead of 0.008 mA** for sleep current is the most common error. Always **show units in every step**.
> 5. **No diagram** — A neat 4-layer block diagram earns at least **2–3 marks** even if the rest is incomplete. KTU examiners reward diagrams.
> 6. **Ignoring stakeholders** — In a Smart City question, mentioning "citizen" or "municipal corporation" shows contextual awareness.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (KTU Module 1 — Application Areas)**

- **Three canonical areas:** Smart Home (personal scale), Smart Agriculture (rural / wide area), Smart City (urban / mass scale).
- **Common 4-layer architecture:** Perception → Network → Processing → Application.
- **Smart Home:** Wi-Fi + ZigBee + BLE; main KPI = *energy saved* and *user comfort*; biggest concern = *interoperability*.
- **Smart Agriculture:** LoRaWAN + NB-IoT; main KPI = *yield per litre of water*; biggest concern = *rural connectivity & power*.
- **Smart City:** 5G + Fiber + LoRa; main KPI = *commute time / AQI / waste-collection efficiency*; biggest concern = *data privacy & scale*.
- **Battery-life formula (memorize):**
  $$ T = \dfrac{C \cdot V}{I_{\text{avg}} \cdot 8760} $$
- **Energy-saving formula:** $ \Delta E = N \times t \times (P_{\text{old}} - P_{\text{new,avg}}) $
- **Mnemonic — HAC** (Home, Agriculture, City) for the three verticals.
- **Key insight:** All three verticals share the same IoT architecture, but the *protocol choice, power strategy, and KPI* differ based on **scale, mobility, and latency** needs.
- **Exam mantra:** Always pair each application with **(i) 2–3 sensors, (ii) 1 communication protocol, (iii) 1 KPI, (iv) 1 challenge**.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — OEAET721 (Internet of Things) — KTU 2024 Scheme.**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET721 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** IoT Hardware

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**IoT Hardware** refers to the physical, tangible electronic components and embedded devices that form the sensing, processing, communication, and actuation layers of an Internet of Things system. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for OEAET721, IoT hardware encompasses the **end-nodes (sensors/actuators), edge devices (microcontrollers/SBCs), communication transceivers, and power subsystems** that collectively enable the perception–computation–transmission pipeline in a connected environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> "IoT Hardware includes the physical components of an IoT system: Sensors, Actuators, Microcontrollers (like Arduino, ESP series), Single Board Computers (like Raspberry Pi), and Communication Modules that collectively sense, process, and transmit data over a network."

The formal architectural definition from the **ISO/IEC 30141:2018 (Internet of Things Reference Architecture)** classifies IoT hardware into the following functional entities:

- **Sensor Entity**: Devices that convert physical phenomena into electrical signals.
- **Actuator Entity**: Devices that convert electrical signals into physical actions.
- **Device Entity**: Computing hardware (MCU/SoC/SBC) that runs the IoT application logic.
- **Communication Entity**: Radio transceivers, antennas, and wired interfaces for data transport.
- **Power Entity**: Battery, energy harvesting, and voltage regulation subsystems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Human Body" Analogy for IoT Hardware**

Imagine the IoT system as a **human body**:
- **Sensors** are the **five senses** (eyes, ears, skin) — they detect the environment (light, temperature, motion).
- **Microcontrollers/SBCs** are the **brain** — they process the sensed information and make decisions.
- **Actuators** are the **muscles and hands** — they perform physical actions based on brain decisions (turn on a fan, open a valve).
- **Communication Modules** are the **nervous system and mouth** — they transmit data to other systems or humans.
- **Power Supply** is the **food and oxygen** — without energy, nothing works.

So, when you say *"turn on the AC when the room temperature exceeds 30°C"*, a **temperature sensor (skin)** senses the heat, the **microcontroller (brain)** compares it to 30°C, the **Wi-Fi module (nervous system)** may notify a cloud server, and the **relay/actuator (muscle)** switches ON the AC compressor. All of this is **IoT Hardware** working in harmony.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following engineering metrics are **mandatory** for any IoT hardware design problem in the KTU exam:

| Metric | Standard Value | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Voltage (Vcc)** | **3.3 V / 5 V** | Standard logic levels for MCUs |
| **ADC Resolution** | **8 / 10 / 12 / 16-bit** | Number of discrete voltage levels |
| **ADC Reference Voltage** | **Vref = 1.1 V / 3.3 V / 5.0 V** | Maximum measurable input voltage |
| **Clock Frequency** | **8 MHz – 1.8 GHz** | Determines processing speed |
| **Power Consumption (Sleep)** | **< 1 µW** | Critical for battery IoT nodes |
| **Wi-Fi Frequency Band** | **2.4 GHz / 5 GHz** | ISM band for IEEE 802.11 |
| **BLE Frequency Band** | **2.4 GHz (40 channels)** | Bluetooth Low Energy |
| **LoRa Frequency Band** | **433 / 868 / 915 MHz** | Sub-GHz long-range |
| **ADC Quantization Formula** | $\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | Where $n$ is the bit depth |
| **Nyquist Sampling Rate** | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling frequency |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 3.3 V and 5 V?**
> These are the de-facto standard logic levels. **TTL (Transistor-Transistor Logic)** uses 5 V (historically from the 74-series ICs), while modern CMOS-based MCUs (ESP32, Raspberry Pi Pico) operate at **3.3 V** to reduce power consumption by ~57% (since $P = V^2/R$).

## 1.4 The Five-Layer Hardware Mapping in IoT

For a KTU 2024 Scheme answer, you MUST map hardware to the IoT architectural layers:

1. **Perception Layer Hardware** → Sensors, Actuators, RFID tags.
2. **Network Layer Hardware** → Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa, Zigbee, Cellular (4G/5G) modules.
3. **Processing Layer Hardware** → MCU (Arduino), SoC (ESP32), SBC (Raspberry Pi).
4. **Application Layer Hardware** → Gateways, Cloud servers (physical data centers).
5. **Business Layer Hardware** → Edge AI accelerators (e.g., Google Coral TPU, Intel Movidius).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of IoT Hardware Components

The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically groups IoT hardware into **four cardinal blocks**. Let us analyze each in depth:

### 2.1.1 Sensors — The "Input Organs" of IoT

A **sensor** is a transducer that converts a physical quantity (temperature, pressure, light, humidity) into an electrical signal (voltage, current, resistance, or digital bits).

**Classification of Sensors (High-Yield for KTU):**

- **Active Sensors** → Require external power (e.g., Radar, LIDAR, Ultrasonic HC-SR04).
- **Passive Sensors** → Self-generating signals (e.g., Thermocouple, Photovoltaic cell, Piezoelectric).
- **Analog Sensors** → Output continuous voltage (e.g., LM35 temperature sensor).
- **Digital Sensors** → Output discrete bits/I²C/SPI (e.g., DHT22, BMP280, DS18B20).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Sensor Selection:**
> - **Accuracy** vs. **Precision** vs. **Resolution** are NOT the same. Accuracy is closeness to true value, precision is repeatability, resolution is the smallest detectable change.
> - **LM35** outputs **10 mV/°C** (linear, analog). **DHT22** outputs digital (digital protocol). **DS18B20** uses **1-Wire** protocol.

### 2.1.2 Actuators — The "Output Organs" of IoT

Actuators convert electrical signals into physical actions. They are the *only* way an IoT system can influence the physical world.

| Actuator Type | Energy Conversion | IoT Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **DC Motor** | Electrical → Mechanical Rotation | Robotic vacuum, smart curtain |
| **Stepper Motor** | Electrical → Precise Angular Steps | 3D printer, CNC |
| **Servo Motor** | Electrical → Precise Angular Position | Robotic arm, door lock |
| **Relay (Electromagnetic)** | Electrical → Magnetic Switch | Smart switch (AC mains control) |
| **Solenoid Valve** | Electrical → Linear Motion | Smart irrigation, gas shutoff |
| **Piezo Buzzer** | Electrical → Sound | Alarms, alerts |
| **LED / OLED Display** | Electrical → Light | Visual feedback |
| **Peltier Module (TEC)** | Electrical → Thermal Gradient | Smart cooler/heater |

### 2.1.3 Microcontrollers (MCU) — The "Brain" of Resource-Constrained IoT

An **MCU** is a single-chip computer containing **CPU, RAM, ROM, I/O peripherals, and ADC/DAC** on one IC. Designed for **real-time, deterministic, low-power** IoT tasks.

**Popular IoT MCUs (Must-Memorize for KTU):**

| MCU | Core | Clock | Flash | RAM | Wi-Fi | Cost (Approx.) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **ATmega328P** (Arduino Uno) | 8-bit AVR | 16 MHz | 32 KB | 2 KB | No | ₹400 |
| **ESP8266** | 32-bit Tensilica L106 | 80–160 MHz | 4 MB | 80 KB | Yes (b/g/n) | ₹150 |
| **ESP32** | Dual-core Xtensa LX6 | 240 MHz | 4 MB | 520 KB | Yes + BLE | ₹350 |
| **STM32 (Blue Pill)** | 32-bit ARM Cortex-M3 | 72 MHz | 64 KB | 20 KB | No | ₹250 |
| **Raspberry Pi Pico (RP2040)** | Dual-core ARM Cortex-M0+ | 133 MHz | 2 MB | 264 KB | No | ₹400 |

### 2.1.4 Single Board Computers (SBC) — The "Edge Server" of IoT

An **SBC** is a complete computer on a single PCB, capable of running a full OS (Linux). Used when IoT tasks require heavy computation (image processing, ML inference, gateway functions).

| SBC | SoC | RAM | OS | Cost (Approx.) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Raspberry Pi 4 Model B** | Broadcom BCM2711 (Cortex-A72) | 1/2/4/8 GB | Raspberry Pi OS (Linux) | ₹4,500 – ₹8,000 |
| **Raspberry Pi Zero 2 W** | Broadcom BCM2710A1 | 512 MB | Raspberry Pi OS | ₹1,500 |
| **BeagleBone Black** | TI AM3358 (Cortex-A8) | 512 MB | Debian | ₹6,000 |
| **NVIDIA Jetson Nano** | Maxwell GPU + Cortex-A57 | 4 GB | Ubuntu Linux | ₹15,000 |
| **Arduino Mega 2560** | ATmega2560 | 8 KB | Arduino (firmware) | ₹900 |

> [!NOTE]
> **MCU vs SBC — The Definitive KTU Exam Answer:**
> - **MCU**: No OS, deterministic, low power (< 1 W), low cost (< ₹500), real-time response in microseconds.
> - **SBC**: Full OS (Linux), high power (3–15 W), expensive, supports multitasking, ML/AI workloads.

### 2.1.5 Communication Modules — The "Voice" of IoT

| Protocol | Frequency | Range | Data Rate | Power | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wi-Fi (802.11 b/g/n)** | 2.4 / 5 GHz | 50 m | 11–600 Mbps | High | Indoor smart home, video |
| **BLE (Bluetooth Low Energy)** | 2.4 GHz | 10 m | 1–2 Mbps | Very Low | Wearables, beacons |
| **Zigbee (802.15.4)** | 2.4 GHz | 100 m | 250 kbps | Low | Mesh sensor networks |
| **LoRa (Long Range)** | Sub-GHz | 2–15 km | 0.3–50 kbps | Very Low | Agriculture, smart city |
| **NB-IoT (LTE Cat-NB1)** | Cellular | 1–10 km | 26 kbps | Low | Smart metering |
| **Sigfox** | Sub-GHz | 30–50 km | 100 bps | Ultra Low | Asset tracking |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Following Equations Appear in 100% of KTU Board Exam Numerical Questions on IoT Hardware.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | ADC Step Size / Resolution | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 2^n \right\rfloor$ | Digital Output Code | Integer |
| 3 | $V_{in} = D \cdot \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | Reconstructed Input Voltage | Volts (V) |
| 4 | $\text{SNR} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio of ideal n-bit ADC | dB |
| 5 | $f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | Nyquist Sampling Theorem | Hz |
| 6 | $P = V \cdot I$ | DC Power Dissipation | Watts (W) |
| 7 | $E = P \cdot t$ | Energy Consumption (battery life) | Joules (J) |
| 8 | $T_{battery} = \dfrac{C_{mAh} \cdot V_{nom}}{P_{load}}$ | Battery Life Estimation | Hours (h) |
| 9 | $R = \rho \cdot \dfrac{L}{A}$ | Resistance of conductive trace (for sensor calcs) | Ohms (Ω) |
| 10 | $V_{out} = V_s \cdot \left( \dfrac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2} \right)$ | Voltage Divider (for sensor signal conditioning) | Volts (V) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Table 2.1:** The use of `\vert` or `\mid` instead of `|` ensures LaTeX delimiters do NOT break markdown table rendering.

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of IoT Hardware

The IoT hardware ecosystem is the **physical substrate** of every modern smart system:

- **Smart Agriculture**: Soil moisture sensors + LoRa + solar-powered MCU nodes → precision irrigation.
- **Healthcare (IoMT)**: Wearable ECG + BLE + cloud analytics → remote patient monitoring.
- **Smart Cities**: Air quality sensors + NB-IoT + dashboards → pollution heat maps.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)**: Vibration sensors + ESP32 + MQTT → predictive maintenance of motors.
- **Smart Home**: PIR motion sensor + Arduino + Wi-Fi relay → automated lighting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hidden "Why":**
> Why does KTU emphasize hardware first? Because *every software decision* (RTOS, MQTT, encryption) is **bounded by the hardware's RAM, clock speed, and power budget**. You cannot run TensorFlow Lite on an ATmega328P with 2 KB RAM. The hardware defines the ceiling of possibility.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: ADC Resolution and Digital Output Code

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU 2024 numerical questions on IoT hardware. It tests whether you understand how analog sensor voltages become digital bits.

### Problem Setup

A **soil moisture sensor** produces an analog output voltage $V_{in} = 1.85 \text{ V}$. This is fed into the **10-bit ADC** of an **ESP32** with a reference voltage $V_{ref} = 3.3 \text{ V}$. Calculate:

(a) The LSB (resolution) of the ADC.
(b) The digital output code $D$.
(c) The SNR of the ADC.
(d) The minimum sampling frequency if the soil moisture signal varies at most at $f_{max} = 500 \text{ Hz}$.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**
- Input Voltage, $V_{in} = 1.85 \text{ V}$
- ADC Resolution, $n = 10 \text{ bits}$
- Reference Voltage, $V_{ref} = 3.3 \text{ V}$
- Maximum Signal Frequency, $f_{max} = 500 \text{ Hz}$

**Step (a): Calculate the LSB (Resolution)**

The LSB is the smallest voltage change the ADC can distinguish. Since the ADC divides the reference voltage $V_{ref}$ into $2^n$ equal steps:

$$
\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
\text{LSB} = \frac{3.3 \text{ V}}{2^{10}} = \frac{3.3}{1024}
$$

$$
\text{LSB} = 0.003223 \text{ V} = 3.223 \text{ mV}
$$

> **Logic:** This means the ESP32's ADC can only "see" changes in 3.223 mV steps. A 1.8 mV change would be **lost** — it would round to the same digital code as 0 mV.

---

**Step (b): Calculate the Digital Output Code $D$**

The digital code is the integer count of how many LSBs fit into the input voltage:

$$
D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 2^n \right\rfloor
$$

Substituting:

$$
D = \left\lfloor \frac{1.85}{3.3} \cdot 1024 \right\rfloor
$$

Computing the fraction:

$$
\frac{1.85}{3.3} = 0.560606...
$$

Multiplying by 1024:

$$
0.560606 \times 1024 = 574.06
$$

Applying the floor function:

$$
D = 574_{10} = \text{0x23E}
$$

> **Logic:** The integer 574 is the raw digital code. The microcontroller's firmware then multiplies this by the LSB to recover the voltage: $574 \times 3.223 \text{ mV} = 1.850 \text{ V}$ (matches!).

**Verification (Inverse Operation):**

$$
V_{reconstructed} = D \times \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = 574 \times \frac{3.3}{1024} = 1.8498 \text{ V} \approx 1.85 \text{ V} \quad \checkmark
$$

---

**Step (c): Calculate the Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)**

For an ideal ADC, the SNR in dB is given by the exact formula:

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76
$$

Substituting $n = 10$:

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \times 10 + 1.76
$$

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 60.2 + 1.76 = 61.96 \text{ dB}
$$

> **Logic:** Each additional bit of resolution contributes ~6 dB of SNR. A 10-bit ADC has ~62 dB SNR, while a 16-bit ADC (used in audio) has ~98 dB SNR.

---

**Step (d): Minimum Sampling Frequency (Nyquist Criterion)**

To faithfully reconstruct a band-limited signal, the sampling rate must be at least twice the maximum signal frequency:

$$
f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}
$$

Substituting:

$$
f_s \geq 2 \times 500 = 1000 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ kHz}
$$

> **Logic:** Soil moisture changes slowly (minutes to hours), so 1 kHz is wildly oversampled. In practice, you might sample at 1 Hz to save power.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Battery Life Calculation for an IoT Node

### Problem Setup

An **ESP32-based air quality monitoring node** consumes an average current of $I_{active} = 80 \text{ mA}$ when transmitting, and $I_{sleep} = 10 \text{ µA}$ when idle. The node wakes up once every $T_{cycle} = 60 \text{ s}$, transmits for $t_{active} = 3 \text{ s}$, and sleeps for the rest. The node is powered by a **Li-Po battery** with capacity $C = 2000 \text{ mAh}$ at $V_{nom} = 3.7 \text{ V}$. Calculate the total energy per day and the expected battery life.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Data:**
- Active current, $I_{active} = 80 \text{ mA} = 0.080 \text{ A}$
- Sleep current, $I_{sleep} = 10 \text{ µA} = 0.000010 \text{ A}$
- Active duration per cycle, $t_{active} = 3 \text{ s}$
- Cycle period, $T_{cycle} = 60 \text{ s}$
- Sleep duration per cycle, $t_{sleep} = 60 - 3 = 57 \text{ s}$
- Battery capacity, $C = 2000 \text{ mAh}$
- Nominal voltage, $V_{nom} = 3.7 \text{ V}$

**Step 1: Calculate Average Current per Cycle**

The average current over one 60-second cycle is the weighted average:

$$
I_{avg} = \frac{I_{active} \cdot t_{active} + I_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep}}{T_{cycle}}
$$

Substituting:

$$
I_{avg} = \frac{(0.080)(3) + (0.000010)(57)}{60}
$$

Computing the numerator:

$$
(0.080)(3) = 0.240 \text{ A·s}
$$

$$
(0.000010)(57) = 0.000570 \text{ A·s}
$$

$$
\text{Sum} = 0.240570 \text{ A·s}
$$

Dividing by 60:

$$
I_{avg} = \frac{0.240570}{60} = 0.0040095 \text{ A} = 4.0095 \text{ mA}
$$

---

**Step 2: Calculate Energy Consumed per Day**

Number of cycles per day:

$$
N_{cycles} = \frac{24 \times 3600}{60} = 1440 \text{ cycles/day}
$$

Total charge consumed per day:

$$
Q_{day} = I_{avg} \times 24 \text{ h} = 0.0040095 \times 24 = 0.09623 \text{ Ah} = 96.23 \text{ mAh}
$$

Total energy per day:

$$
E_{day} = Q_{day} \times V_{nom} = 0.09623 \times 3.7 = 0.356 \text{ Wh} = 1281.6 \text{ J}
$$

---

**Step 3: Calculate Battery Life**

$$
T_{battery} = \frac{C_{mAh}}{I_{avg,mA}} = \frac{2000}{4.0095}
$$

$$
T_{battery} = 498.8 \text{ hours} = \frac{498.8}{24} = 20.78 \text{ days}
$$

> **Logic:** By sleeping 95% of the time, the node achieves ~21 days of operation on a single 2000 mAh charge. If it transmitted continuously ($I = 80$ mA), it would last only $2000/80 = 25$ hours.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: MCU vs SBC vs SoC

This matrix is a **favourite KTU 14-mark question** framework. Memorize it thoroughly.

| Parameter | **Microcontroller (MCU)** | **Single Board Computer (SBC)** | **System-on-Chip (SoC)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Examples** | ATmega328, STM32, PIC | Raspberry Pi, BeagleBone | ESP32, CC2530, nRF52840 |
| **OS Required** | No (bare metal / RTOS) | Yes (Linux) | No / Lightweight RTOS |
| **Clock Speed** | 1–500 MHz | 700 MHz – 2 GHz | 80–240 MHz |
| **RAM** | 2 KB – 1 MB | 512 MB – 8 GB | 100 KB – 1 MB |
| **Power** | 0.01 – 0.5 W | 3 – 15 W | 0.05 – 0.8 W |
| **Cost** | ₹100 – ₹500 | ₹1,500 – ₹8,000 | ₹150 – ₹600 |
| **Boot Time** | < 100 ms | 10 – 60 s | < 200 ms |
| **Real-Time** | Yes (deterministic) | No (non-deterministic Linux) | Yes |
| **Connectivity** | External module | USB dongle / onboard | Onboard Wi-Fi/BLE |
| **Best For** | Sensing, low-power nodes | Gateway, ML inference, video | Smart connected sensors |
| **Programming** | C, Arduino sketch, MicroPython | Python, C++, Node.js | ESP-IDF, Arduino, MicroPython |

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison: Analog vs Digital Sensors

| Parameter | **Analog Sensor** | **Digital Sensor** |
|---|---|---|
| **Output** | Continuous voltage (0–Vref) | Discrete bits / I²C / SPI / 1-Wire |
| **Resolution** | Limited by ADC bit depth | Native bit depth (e.g., 16-bit) |
| **Noise Susceptibility** | High (long wires pick up EMI) | Low (digital encoding) |
| **Calibration** | Required (drift over time) | Factory calibrated (e.g., BME280) |
| **Examples** | LM35 (temp), TMP36 | DHT22, DS18B20, BME280 |
| **Cost** | Lower (₹30–₹100) | Higher (₹150–₹500) |
| **Wiring** | 3 wires (VCC, GND, OUT) | 2–4 wires (VCC, GND, DATA, CLK) |
| **KTU Exam Tip** | Needs ADC + scaling equation | Needs protocol (I²C/SPI) driver |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern Note:** Part A questions in the KTU 2024 ESE are compulsory, each carrying **3 marks**, and typically test direct definitions, comparisons, or single-line derivations. The expected answer length is **60–90 words** plus a small diagram or equation where applicable.

---

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define an IoT sensor. Differentiate between active and passive sensors with one example each.**

### Model Answer (3 Marks):

> An **IoT sensor** is a transducer device that detects a physical, chemical, or biological quantity (temperature, humidity, motion, gas concentration) and converts it into an electrical signal interpretable by a microcontroller.
>
> - **Active Sensor**: Requires an external power source to function. Example: **Ultrasonic sensor HC-SR04** (needs 5V to emit and receive ultrasonic pulses).
> - **Passive Sensor**: Self-generates an electrical signal in response to the stimulus. Example: **Thermocouple** (generates a millivolt-level voltage from a temperature gradient, no external power needed for sensing).
>
> *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for active sensor with example, 1 mark for passive sensor with example.)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Explain the term "Single Board Computer (SBC)" with an example. How is it different from a microcontroller?**

### Model Answer (3 Marks):

> A **Single Board Computer (SBC)** is a complete computer built on a single printed circuit board, including a processor, RAM, storage, I/O ports, and network interfaces, capable of running a full **operating system** like Linux. Example: **Raspberry Pi 4 Model B** (Broadcom BCM2711, 4 GB RAM, runs Raspberry Pi OS).
>
> **Key Difference from a Microcontroller:**
>
> | Feature | SBC | MCU |
> |---|---|---|
> | OS | Full OS (Linux) | Bare metal / RTOS |
> | Power | 3–15 W | 0.01–0.5 W |
> | Cost | ₹1,500+ | ₹100–₹500 |
> | Boot Time | 10–60 s | < 100 ms |
>
> *(1 mark SBC + example, 2 marks comparison.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Part B questions have internal choice (Answer ANY ONE from Q-A and Q-B). Each 14-mark question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts: typically (a) theoretical/derivation and (b) comparison/design/numerical.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### **Q. (a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an IoT node. Identify and describe the function of each hardware block.

#### **Q. (b) [7 Marks]** An IoT node uses a 12-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5.0 \text{ V}$ to read a pressure sensor. If the sensor output voltage is $V_{in} = 3.72 \text{ V}$, calculate the (i) LSB, (ii) digital output code, (iii) the percentage quantization error, and (iv) the SNR of the ADC.

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: IoT Node Architecture**

An **IoT node** (also called an end-device or smart sensor) is the fundamental hardware unit that performs sensing, processing, and communication. Its architecture consists of **five inter-connected hardware blocks**:

**1. Sensor / Actuator Block (1 Mark):**
The interface with the physical world. Sensors (LM35, DHT22) convert physical parameters into electrical signals. Actuators (relays, motors) convert electrical commands into physical actions.

**2. Processing Block (MCU/SBC) (2 Marks):**
The "brain" of the node. A **microcontroller (e.g., ESP32, ATmega328P)** runs the application firmware, performs ADC conversion, executes control algorithms, and manages data flow. For compute-intensive tasks (video, ML), a **Single Board Computer (Raspberry Pi)** is used.

**3. Communication Block (1 Mark):**
The radio transceiver (Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa, Zigbee) enables wireless data transmission to gateways, smartphones, or the cloud. The antenna and matching network are part of this block.

**4. Power Block (1 Mark):**
Provides regulated DC voltage to all blocks. May include a **Li-ion/Li-Po battery, solar panel, energy harvester, and buck-boost regulators (e.g., AMS1117-3.3V, BQ25504).**

**5. Storage and User Interface Block (1 Mark):**
Optional local storage (EEPROM, SD card) for buffering, and user interfaces (LEDs, buttons, OLED displays) for status and interaction.

**Block Diagram Flow:**

```
[Physical World] → [Sensor] → [Signal Conditioning] → [ADC] → [MCU] → [Comm Module] → [Network]
                        ↑                                                                    ↓
                   [Actuator] ← [GPIO Control] ← [MCU] ← ← ← [Cloud/Server]
```

**Data Flow Description (1 Mark):**
- The sensor converts a physical quantity (e.g., temperature) into an analog voltage.
- The analog signal is **conditioned** (amplified, filtered) and digitized by the **ADC**.
- The MCU processes the digital value, runs control logic, and packages it (e.g., JSON, MQTT).
- The communication module transmits the data wirelessly to a gateway.
- Reverse flow: Cloud commands are received → MCU parses → GPIO toggles → Actuator responds.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: ADC Numerical**

**Given:**
- Resolution $n = 12$ bits
- Reference voltage $V_{ref} = 5.0 \text{ V}$
- Input voltage $V_{in} = 3.72 \text{ V}$

**Sub-part (i): LSB Calculation (2 Marks)**

The least significant bit is the smallest voltage change the ADC can resolve:

$$
\text{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} = \frac{5.0}{2^{12}} = \frac{5.0}{4096} = 0.001221 \text{ V} = 1.221 \text{ mV}
$$

**Sub-part (ii): Digital Output Code (2 Marks)**

$$
D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot 2^n \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \frac{3.72}{5.0} \cdot 4096 \right\rfloor
$$

$$
D = \left\lfloor 0.744 \times 4096 \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 3047.66 \right\rfloor = 3047_{10} = \text{0xBE7}
$$

**Sub-part (iii): Percentage Quantization Error (1 Mark)**

Quantization error is bounded by $\pm \frac{1}{2} \text{LSB}$:

$$
\text{Error}_{\%} = \frac{\text{LSB}}{V_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{0.001221}{3.72} \times 100 = 0.0328\%
$$

**Sub-part (iv): SNR of the ADC (2 Marks)**

For an ideal ADC, the signal-to-noise ratio is:

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0 \text{ dB}
$$

**Final Answer Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| LSB | **1.221 mV** |
| Digital Code | **3047 (0xBE7)** |
| Quantization Error | **0.0328 %** |
| SNR | **74.0 dB** |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### **Q. (a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the architectures of Arduino Uno, ESP32, and Raspberry Pi 4 for IoT applications. Recommend the most suitable platform for: (i) a battery-powered soil moisture sensor, (ii) an AI-based face recognition doorbell.

#### **Q. (b) [7 Marks]** Explain the major categories of IoT sensors with examples. Discuss the working principle of a DHT22 digital temperature-humidity sensor in detail.

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks: IoT Platform Comparison**

| Parameter | **Arduino Uno** | **ESP32** | **Raspberry Pi 4** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Core** | 8-bit ATmega328P (AVR) | 32-bit Dual Core Xtensa LX6 | 64-bit Quad Core Cortex-A72 |
| **Clock** | 16 MHz | 240 MHz | 1.5 GHz |
| **Flash / Storage** | 32 KB | 4 MB | microSD up to 1 TB |
| **RAM** | 2 KB SRAM | 520 KB SRAM | 1/2/4/8 GB LPDDR4 |
| **Wi-Fi / BLE** | No (need shield) | Yes (built-in) | Yes (built-in) |
| **Power** | ~50 mA (5 V) = 0.25 W | ~80 mA active = 0.26 W | ~600 mA (5 V) = 3 W |
| **OS** | None (bare metal) | None / FreeRTOS | Raspberry Pi OS (Linux) |
| **Cost** | ₹400 | ₹350 | ₹4,500 – ₹8,000 |
| **Boot Time** | < 100 ms | < 200 ms | 15–60 s |
| **GPIO Pins** | 14 digital + 6 analog | 34 digital + 18 analog | 40 (via header) |
| **Best For** | Hobby, learning | Wi-Fi IoT nodes | Gateway, ML, multimedia |

**Recommendations (2 Marks):**

- **(i) Battery-Powered Soil Moisture Sensor:** **ESP32** is best. It has built-in Wi-Fi for data upload, supports deep sleep (10 µA), and is cheap. Arduino Uno is cheaper but needs a separate Wi-Fi shield (₹800+), which increases both cost and power consumption. Raspberry Pi is overkill (3 W power, 60 s boot time, ₹8,000 cost) — the battery would die in hours.

- **(ii) AI-Based Face Recognition Doorbell:** **Raspberry Pi 4** is the correct choice. Face recognition using OpenCV + dlib requires ~1 GB RAM and a multi-core processor, which Arduino (2 KB RAM) and ESP32 (520 KB RAM) cannot provide. The Raspberry Pi can run TensorFlow Lite or OpenCV and process 5–10 FPS video at the doorbell camera input.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks: IoT Sensor Categories & DHT22 Working**

**Categories of IoT Sensors (3 Marks):**

1. **Temperature Sensors** — LM35 (analog), DS18B20 (1-Wire digital), DHT22.
2. **Humidity Sensors** — DHT22, SHT31 (I²C), HIH-8120.
3. **Motion / Position Sensors** — PIR (HC-SR501), Accelerometer (MPU6050), Gyroscope.
4. **Light Sensors** — LDR (photoresistor), BH1750 (I²C lux), TSL2561.
5. **Gas / Chemical Sensors** — MQ-2 (LPG/smoke), MQ-135 (air quality), SGP30 (VOC/eCO₂).
6. **Proximity / Distance Sensors** — HC-SR04 (ultrasonic), VL53L0X (ToF laser).
7. **Pressure Sensors** — BMP280, BME280 (also measures temp and humidity).
8. **Biometric Sensors** — MAX30102 (heart rate, SpO₂), AD8232 (ECG).

**DHT22 Working Principle (4 Marks):**

The **DHT22 (AM2302)** is a digital temperature and humidity sensor that uses a **capacitive humidity sensor** and a **thermistor** for measurement, with a built-in 8-bit MCU for signal processing.

**Step 1: Sensing Mechanism (1 Mark)**
- **Humidity Sensing**: A polymer dielectric layer between two electrodes forms a capacitor. As humidity changes, the polymer absorbs/releases water molecules, changing the capacitance. The MCU measures this capacitance change.
- **Temperature Sensing**: An NTC (Negative Temperature Coefficient) thermistor's resistance decreases as temperature rises. The MCU measures the voltage across a precision resistor-thermistor divider.

**Step 2: Data Encoding (2 Marks)**
- The internal MCU converts both measurements into 16-bit digital values each.
- The complete data frame is **40 bits long**: 16-bit humidity + 16-bit temperature + 8-bit checksum.
- Data is transmitted over a single-wire protocol (proprietary, similar to 1-Wire).

**Step 3: Communication Protocol (1 Mark)**
- The MCU sends a **start signal** (low for 1 ms) to wake the DHT22.
- The DHT22 responds with a **handshake** (low 80 µs, high 80 µs).
- Data bits are encoded: a **50 µs low + 26–28 µs high = '0'**, and a **50 µs low + 70 µs high = '1'**.
- The ESP32/Arduino reads the pulse widths using `micros()` or hardware timers.

**Step 4: Specifications**
- **Range**: Temperature −40 to 80 °C (±0.5 °C), Humidity 0–100 % RH (±2 %).
- **Sampling rate**: 0.5 Hz (one reading every 2 seconds).
- **Operating voltage**: 3.0–5.5 V.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cause Mark Deductions in KTU 2024 IoT Hardware Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing MCU with SBC** — Many students write "Arduino is a Single Board Computer". It is NOT. Arduino Uno is a **microcontroller development board**. Only Raspberry Pi, BeagleBone, and Jetson Nano are SBCs. This is a **direct 2-mark deduction**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to include units in ADC formulas** — Writing $\text{LSB} = 3.3/1024 = 0.00322$ without writing "Volts" loses **0.5 marks**. KTU examiners strictly check units.
>
> 3. **Confusing active and passive sensors** — A thermocouple is a **passive sensor** (self-generating), NOT active. A photodiode in **photovoltaic mode** is passive, but in **photoconductive mode** (reverse biased) is **active**. Students often mix these up.
>
> 4. **Using `|` inside markdown tables** — The vertical pipe character breaks markdown table rendering. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in your exam scratch work if you copy tables.
>
> 5. **Writing "Wi-Fi module" instead of "ESP32 Wi-Fi SoC"** — Wi-Fi is a protocol, not a hardware component. The hardware is the **transceiver IC (ESP32, CC3100)**. Examiners expect this distinction.
>
> 6. **Skipping the Nyquist criterion** — In sensor sampling problems, ALWAYS mention the Nyquist sampling rate $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ even if not explicitly asked. It shows conceptual understanding and often earns a bonus mark.
>
> 7. **Not showing the floor function in ADC code** — Writing $D = V_{in} \cdot 2^n / V_{ref}$ without the $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ notation loses 0.5 marks. The truncation is essential.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for IoT Hardware (KTU OEAET721 Module 2)**

- **Definition**: IoT Hardware = physical components (sensors + actuators + MCUs/SBCs + communication modules + power) that sense, process, and transmit data.
- **5 Architectural Layers** (ISO/IEC 30141): Perception → Network → Processing → Application → Business.
- **Sensor Classes**:
  - Active vs Passive (power requirement).
  - Analog vs Digital (output type).
  - Examples: LM35 (analog T), DHT22 (digital T+RH), HC-SR04 (ultrasonic distance), MQ-2 (gas).
- **Actuator Classes**:
  - Electrical → Mechanical (DC motor, servo, stepper).
  - Electrical → Thermal (Peltier, heater).
  - Electrical → Magnetic (relay, solenoid).
  - Electrical → Optical (LED, OLED, laser).
- **MCU vs SBC**:
  - MCU: no OS, < 1 W, < ₹500, real-time.
  - SBC: full OS, 3–15 W, > ₹1,500, ML/video.
  - Examples: ATmega328P vs Raspberry Pi 4.
- **Popular IoT SoCs**:
  - ESP8266 (Wi-Fi only, 80 MHz, ₹150).
  - ESP32 (Wi-Fi + BLE, 240 MHz dual-core, ₹350).
  - **ESP32 is the most important KTU-recommended IoT hardware**.
- **Communication Modules**:
  - Short range: Wi-Fi, BLE, Zigbee.
  - Long range: LoRa, NB-IoT, Sigfox.
  - Frequency bands: 2.4 GHz (Wi-Fi/BLE/Zigbee), Sub-GHz (LoRa).
- **Key Formulas** (Memorize Without Exception):
  - $\text{LSB} = V_{ref} / 2^n$
  - $D = \lfloor V_{in} \cdot 2^n / V_{ref} \rfloor$
  - $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02n + 1.76$
  - $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist)
  - $I_{avg} = \frac{I_{active} t_{active} + I_{sleep} t_{sleep}}{T_{cycle}}$ (Battery life)
- **ADC Bit Depth Reference Table**:
  - 8-bit → 256 levels → 12.9 mV at 3.3 V.
  - 10-bit → 1024 levels → 3.22 mV at 3.3 V.
  - 12-bit → 4096 levels → 0.806 mV at 3.3 V.
  - 16-bit → 65536 levels → 0.05 mV at 3.3 V.
- **Power Optimization Techniques**:
  - Sleep modes (ESP32 deep sleep: 10 µA).
  - Duty cycling (active 5% of time, sleep 95%).
  - Energy harvesting (solar, piezoelectric, thermal).
  - Voltage scaling (3.3 V vs 5 V).
- **I²C vs SPI vs UART** (Must Know for 7-Mark Questions):
  - **I²C**: 2 wires (SDA, SCL), multiple slaves via address, up to 400 kHz.
  - **SPI**: 4 wires (MOSI, MISO, SCK, CS), fast (up to 80 MHz), full duplex.
  - **UART**: 2 wires (TX, RX), asynchronous, point-to-point, 9600–115200 baud typical.
- **Voltage Divider for Sensor Calibration** (signal conditioning):
$$V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot \frac{R_2}{R_1 + R_2}$$
- **KTU Topper's Tip**: Always draw a **block diagram** before solving any IoT hardware problem. Examiners award up to **2 extra marks** for a well-labeled block diagram in 14-mark answers.
- **Commonly Asked Numericals** (Practice These):
  1. 10-bit ADC with 3.3 V reference and 1.85 V input.
  2. 12-bit ADC with 5.0 V reference and 3.72 V input.
  3. Battery life with active and sleep current duty cycle.
  4. Nyquist sampling rate calculation.
