> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET721 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Commercially Available Sensors (Operating Principles Only) - Temperature and Humidity Sensor (DHT11/22), Gas Sensor (MQ Series), Ultrasonic Distance Sensor (HC-SR04)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are "Sensors" in the Context of IoT?

In the Internet of Things (IoT) architecture, a **sensor** is a physical device that detects a change in an environmental condition (the *stimulus*) and converts that change into a measurable electrical signal (the *response*). These devices form the **Perception Layer** — the lowest and most critical tier of the standard 5-layer IoT stack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** A sensor is an electronic component that **transduces** a physical, chemical, or biological quantity into an electrical signal (voltage, current, resistance, or frequency) which can be conditioned, digitized, and transmitted over a network.

**Transduction Principle Categories** (relevant to our three sensors):

| Sensor Type | Stimulus Detected | Transduction Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| DHT11 / DHT22 | Temperature + Humidity | Capacitive (humidity) + Resistive / NTC (temperature) |
| MQ Series (e.g., MQ-2, MQ-135) | Various gases (LPG, CO₂, NH₃) | Chemi-resistive (semiconductor surface reaction) |
| HC-SR04 | Distance to object | Acoustic Time-of-Flight (piezoelectric) |

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogies (Plain English for First-Time Learners)

### 🍳 Analogy for DHT11 / DHT22 (Capacitive Humidity + NTC Temperature)
Imagine a **sponge sandwich** between two metal plates — when the air gets humid, the sponge soaks up water and stores charge differently. The "stuffiness" of the sponge to electricity tells you the humidity. For temperature, imagine a **wire that loses its grip on electricity as it gets hot** — like a tired runner slowing down. The hotter it is, the *less* the wire resists current. This is a *Negative Temperature Coefficient* (NTC) effect.

### 🚗 Analogy for MQ Series Gas Sensors
Think of a **tiny hot plate** inside the sensor with a special coating. When the air is clean, oxygen sticks to the coating and "steals" electrons, making it a poor conductor. When a target gas (say LPG leak from a kitchen) shows up, the gas molecules **kick off the oxygen** and **return the stolen electrons**, making the surface conduct electricity much better. The change in current is your gas concentration reading.

### 🦇 Analogy for HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Sensor
Bats navigate caves by **shouting a chirp and listening for the echo**. HC-SR04 does the same — it sends out a 40 kHz "chirp" too high-pitched for human ears, then waits for the bounce-back. The longer it waits, the further away the object. The microcontroller does the math: `Distance = (Echo Time × Speed of Sound) ÷ 2`.

---

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The course explicitly requires students to know the **operating principles only** for these three sensor families. This means you must explain *how* they work physically, not how to program them. Manufacturer datasheets (Aosong for DHT, Winsen for MQ, Elec Freaks for HC-SR04) are the standard references.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 DHT11 vs DHT22 — Operating Principle

The DHT11 and DHT22 are **factory-calibrated, single-wire digital-output** sensors. Internally, they house **two separate sensing elements**:

### A. Humidity Sensing Element (Capacitive Polymer)

- A **thin polymer dielectric film** is sandwiched between two conductive electrodes, forming a capacitor $C_h$.
- The polymer absorbs/desorbs water molecules based on ambient relative humidity (RH).
- The dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$ of the polymer **changes linearly** with absorbed moisture.
- For a parallel-plate capacitor:

$$
C_h = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}{d}
$$

Where:
- $C_h$ = humidity-dependent capacitance
- $\varepsilon_0$ = permittivity of free space $\approx 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m
- $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity of the polymer (a function of RH)
- $A$ = electrode area; $d$ = dielectric thickness

A small onboard ASIC converts the change in $C_h$ into a digital RH value.

### B. Temperature Sensing Element (NTC Thermistor)

- A **Negative Temperature Coefficient (NTC) thermistor** is a semiconductor whose resistance **decreases** as temperature rises.
- The governing equation is the **Steinhart-Hart equation** (simplified as the B-parameter or $\beta$-equation):

$$
\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_0} + \frac{1}{\beta} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{R}{R_0}\right)
$$

Where:
- $T$ = absolute temperature in Kelvin (current)
- $T_0$ = reference temperature (typically **298.15 K** = 25 °C)
- $R$ = thermistor resistance at $T$
- $R_0$ = thermistor resistance at $T_0$ (commonly **10 kΩ**)
- $\beta$ = material constant (typically **3950** for DHT22)

### C. Single-Wire Communication
- Both humidity and temperature are transmitted as **40-bit data packets** (16-bit RH + 16-bit T + 8-bit checksum).
- Protocol: open-drain with pull-up; MCU initiates by pulling line LOW for $\geq 18$ ms.

### D. DHT11 vs DHT22 Comparison

| Parameter | DHT11 | DHT22 |
|---|---|---|
| Humidity Range | 20 – 90 % RH (5 % accuracy) | 0 – 100 % RH (2–5 % accuracy) |
| Temperature Range | 0 – 50 °C (± 2 °C) | -40 to 80 °C (± 0.5 °C) |
| Resolution | 8-bit (1 °C / 1 % RH) | 16-bit (0.1 °C / 0.1 % RH) |
| Sampling Rate | 1 Hz | 0.5 Hz |
| Cost | Low | Moderate |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** DHT11 is used in hobbyist weather stations and indoor air-quality monitors. DHT22 (also called AM2302) is preferred in precision agriculture, greenhouse automation, and HVAC systems.

---

## 2.2 MQ Series Gas Sensors — Operating Principle

The MQ series (e.g., **MQ-2** for LPG/propane/smoke, **MQ-5** for LPG/natural gas, **MQ-7** for carbon monoxide, **MQ-135** for air quality / NH₃ / NOₓ) are **chemi-resistive metal-oxide-semiconductor (MOS) sensors**.

### Internal Architecture (6-Pin Package)
- A **ceramic substrate** with embedded **heater coil** (Ni-Cr alloy, $\approx 33 \pm 5$ Ω)
- A **SnO₂ (tin dioxide) sensing layer** deposited on a tube
- Two **measuring electrodes** + two **heater pins**

### Working Mechanism (Step-by-Step)
1. **Heating phase:** The coil is powered (typically 5 V) and heats the SnO₂ to **200 – 400 °C** depending on the model. This is essential — without heat, the sensor is inactive.
2. **Clean air baseline:** At operating temperature, atmospheric **oxygen adsorbs on the SnO₂ surface**, capturing free electrons from the conduction band. This creates a depletion layer and forms a **potential barrier**, leaving the SnO₂ in a **high-resistance state**.
3. **Gas exposure:** The target gas (e.g., LPG) reacts with the adsorbed oxygen, forming **CO₂ and H₂O**. This **releases the trapped electrons** back into the conduction band.
4. **Conductance change:** The sensor's resistance $R_s$ **drops dramatically** when reducing gases are present. The amount of drop correlates with gas concentration.

### Key Equations

The MQ sensor's behavior is characterized by a **sensitivity curve** given by the ratio $R_s/R_0$, where $R_0$ is the sensor resistance at a reference gas concentration (e.g., 1000 ppm LPG for MQ-2).

$$
R_s = R_0 \cdot f(C_{gas})
$$

A common empirical form (used by manufacturers):

$$
\frac{R_s}{R_0} = A \cdot (C_{gas})^{-B}
$$

Where:
- $C_{gas}$ = concentration in ppm
- $A, B$ = empirical constants (e.g., for MQ-2 in LPG: $A \approx 10$, $B \approx 0.6$)

For load-resistor-based voltage reading (typical interface circuit):

$$
V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_s + R_L}
$$

Where $R_L$ is the external load resistor (commonly **10 kΩ**).

### Power and Heater Requirements

| Sensor | Heater Voltage | Heater Power | Preheat Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| MQ-2 | 5 V AC/DC | $\approx 800$ mW | 24 – 48 hours initial burn-in |
| MQ-7 | 5 V (1 min) + 1.4 V (cool-down) | $\approx 350$ mW | 48 hours |
| MQ-135 | 5 V | $\approx 800$ mW | 24 – 48 hours |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** MQ-2 in domestic LPG leak detectors; MQ-135 in outdoor pollution monitoring stations; MQ-7 in underground parking CO detection systems.

---

## 2.3 HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Distance Sensor — Operating Principle

The HC-SR04 measures distance by emitting an **ultrasonic burst** and timing the echo return. It contains:
- One **piezoelectric transmitter** (emits sound)
- One **piezoelectric receiver** (detects echo)
- An onboard **driver + amplifier circuit**

### Working Sequence
1. **Trigger:** MCU sends a **10 µs HIGH pulse** on the TRIG pin.
2. **Burst emission:** The sensor internally emits **eight 40 kHz pulses** ($\lambda \approx 8.5$ mm in air).
3. **Propagation:** Sound waves travel through air at the **speed of sound** $v$ until they strike an object.
4. **Reflection:** Object reflects the sound; the receiver picks it up.
5. **Echo pulse:** The ECHO pin is held HIGH for a duration equal to the round-trip time $t$.
6. **MCU calculation:** Distance is computed from $t$.

### Governing Equation (Time-of-Flight)

$$
d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}
$$

Where:
- $d$ = one-way distance to object (m)
- $v$ = speed of sound in air (m/s)
- $t$ = ECHO pin HIGH time (s)
- The factor **2** accounts for the round trip (there and back)

The speed of sound in air is temperature-dependent:

$$
v = 331.3 + 0.606 \cdot T \quad \text{(m/s)}
$$

Where $T$ is air temperature in **°C**. At $T = 20$ °C, $v \approx 343.3$ m/s.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pitfall:** Students often forget the **÷ 2** factor. The echo time is for the *round trip*, so the distance to the object is *half* the total distance traveled by the sound wave. Board examiners specifically test this!

### Effective Range and Limitations
- Minimum measurable distance: **2 cm** (shorter echoes are missed)
- Maximum measurable distance: **400 cm** (4 m) — limited by echo attenuation
- Accuracy: **± 3 mm** under ideal conditions
- Blind spot: objects closer than 2 cm are *not detected* at all
- Beam pattern: $\approx 15°$ cone angle
- **Cannot detect soft/angled surfaces** reliably (sound is absorbed or reflected away from the sensor)

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Summary Table)

| Symbol | Formula / Meaning | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C_h$ | $\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ (humidity capacitor) | 30 – 60 pF | F |
| $R_{NTC}$ | NTC thermistor resistance, Steinhart-Hart law | 10 kΩ at 25 °C | Ω |
| $\beta$ | Thermistor material constant | 3950 | K (kelvin) |
| $R_s$ | MQ sensor surface resistance | 3 kΩ – 30 kΩ | Ω |
| $R_0$ | MQ reference resistance (clean air) | varies by model | Ω |
| $R_s / R_0$ | Sensitivity ratio (log-scale with ppm) | depends on gas | dimensionless |
| $V_{out}$ | $V_{cc} \cdot R_L / (R_s + R_L)$ | 0 – 5 V | V |
| $d$ | $v \cdot t / 2$ (HC-SR04 distance) | 0.02 – 4.0 m | m |
| $v$ | $331.3 + 0.606 \cdot T$ (speed of sound) | 343 m/s at 20 °C | m/s |
| $f_{US}$ | Ultrasonic frequency of HC-SR04 | 40 000 | Hz |
| $\lambda$ | Wavelength in air ($\lambda = v / f$) | 8.5 mm | m |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Capacitive Humidity Sensor Sensitivity

**Goal:** Show how a change in relative humidity (RH %) produces a measurable change in capacitance.

**Step 1.** Start with the parallel-plate capacitance model:

$$
C_h = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}{d}
$$

**Step 2.** The polymer's dielectric constant varies with RH. For a typical polyimide sensing film, a linear approximation holds for moderate ranges:

$$
\varepsilon_r(RH) \approx \varepsilon_{r,0} + k \cdot RH
$$

**Step 3.** Substitute into the capacitance equation:

$$
C_h(RH) = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot (\varepsilon_{r,0} + k \cdot RH) \cdot A}{d}
$$

**Step 4.** The change in capacitance from a reference (typically $RH_0 = 55\%$) is:

$$
\Delta C_h = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot k \cdot A}{d} \cdot (RH - RH_0)
$$

**Step 5.** The **sensitivity** $S$ (capacitance change per percent RH) is therefore:

$$
S = \frac{\partial C_h}{\partial (RH)} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot k \cdot A}{d}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The derivative isolates the variable we care about. For a fixed sensor geometry ($A$ and $d$), the polymer's $k$ value (moisture absorption coefficient) directly controls sensitivity — this is why manufacturers spend years optimizing the polymer chemistry.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: NTC Thermistor — Resistance vs Temperature

**Goal:** Find the resistance $R$ of a DHT22's NTC thermistor at an arbitrary temperature $T$ given the reference values.

**Given (typical DHT22 NTC element):**
- $R_0 = 10$ kΩ at $T_0 = 298.15$ K (i.e., 25 °C)
- $\beta = 3950$ K

**Step 1.** The B-parameter equation (form of Steinhart-Hart) is:

$$
\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_0} + \frac{1}{\beta} \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{R}{R_0}\right)
$$

**Step 2.** Solve for $R$:

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{R}{R_0}\right) = \beta \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)
$$

**Step 3.** Exponentiate both sides:

$$
\frac{R}{R_0} = \exp\!\left[\beta \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 4.** Therefore:

$$
R = R_0 \cdot \exp\!\left[\beta \cdot \left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]
$$

**Numerical Example:** What is $R$ at $T = 318.15$ K (i.e., 45 °C)?

**Step 5.** Compute the bracketed term:

$$
\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{318.15} - \frac{1}{298.15}
$$

**Step 6.** Evaluate:

$$
\frac{1}{318.15} \approx 3.1432 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{298.15} \approx 3.3540 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 7.** Subtract:

$$
3.1432 \times 10^{-3} - 3.3540 \times 10^{-3} = -2.108 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 8.** Multiply by $\beta = 3950$:

$$
3950 \times (-2.108 \times 10^{-4}) \approx -0.8327
$$

**Step 9.** Exponentiate:

$$
\exp(-0.8327) \approx 0.4348
$$

**Step 10.** Multiply by $R_0 = 10$ kΩ:

$$
R = 10 \, \text{k}\Omega \times 0.4348 \approx 4.348 \, \text{k}\Omega
$$

> **Conversion logic:** The result (4.35 kΩ at 45 °C) is **less than 10 kΩ**, confirming the NTC behavior — resistance **falls** as temperature **rises**. The DHT22's onboard ASIC measures this resistance via a voltage divider and digitizes it for the single-wire protocol.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: HC-SR04 Distance Calculation

**Goal:** Given a measured echo time $t = 588$ µs at $T = 20$ °C, calculate distance $d$.

**Step 1.** Speed of sound in air at 20 °C:

$$
v = 331.3 + 0.606 \times 20 = 331.3 + 12.12 = 343.42 \, \text{m/s}
$$

**Step 2.** Use the time-of-flight equation:

$$
d = \frac{v \cdot t}{2}
$$

**Step 3.** Convert $t$ to seconds: $t = 588 \times 10^{-6}$ s.

**Step 4.** Compute the total round-trip distance:

$$
v \cdot t = 343.42 \times 588 \times 10^{-6} = 0.20193 \, \text{m}
$$

**Step 5.** Divide by 2 (one-way distance):

$$
d = \frac{0.20193}{2} \approx 0.10096 \, \text{m} \approx 10.1 \, \text{cm}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** A standard test target is placed exactly 10 cm from the sensor. The reading of ~10.1 cm verifies operation with sub-millimeter error.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Sensor

| Requirement | Best Sensor | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Continuous environmental monitoring (weather station) | **DHT22** | High accuracy, wide range, digital output |
| LPG leak detection in a kitchen | **MQ-2** | Sensitive to propane/butane, cheap |
| Car-parking CO monitor | **MQ-7** | CO-selective, alternating high/low heater cycle |
| Liquid level in a tank | **HC-SR04** | Non-contact, works through plastic walls |
| Air pollution outdoor station | **MQ-135** | Detects NH₃, NOₓ, CO₂, smoke |
| Human-presence detection in a room | **DHT11** (loosely, for human comfort) | Cheap, basic RH/T |
| Robotic obstacle avoidance | **HC-SR04** | Real-time, range-measuring, fast updates |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: DHT11 / DHT22 Internal Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Ambient Air] --> nodeB[Polymer Dielectric Film]
    nodeA --> nodeC[NTC Thermistor]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Capacitance Change dC]
    nodeC --> nodeE[Resistance Change dR]
    nodeD --> nodeF[ASIC Sigma Delta ADC]
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG[Microcontroller Single Wire Bus]
    nodeG --> nodeH[40 Bit Digital Packet]
    nodeH --> nodeI[16 bit RH plus 16 bit T plus 8 bit Checksum]
    style nodeA fill:#FFD580
    style nodeH fill:#90EE90
    style nodeI fill:#87CEEB
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Ambient air simultaneously acts on the polymer (changing its capacitance) and the NTC (changing its resistance). Both signals are converted by the on-board ASIC into a single 40-bit digital packet that the MCU reads on one GPIO pin.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: MQ Series Gas Sensor Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[5V DC Supply] --> nodeB[Heater Coil NiCr Alloy]
    nodeB --> nodeC[SnO2 Sensing Layer Heated to 300 C]
    nodeD[Atmospheric Oxygen] --> nodeE[Adsorbed O minus on SnO2 Surface]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Electron Trapping High Resistance State]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Target Gas Reaches Sensor]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Redox Reaction Releases Electrons]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Conductance Increases Rs Drops]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Voltage Divider RL Plus Rs]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[Analog Output to ADC]
    style nodeC fill:#FFB6C1
    style nodeI fill:#FFD700
    style nodeK fill:#98FB98
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Without gas, oxygen-starved SnO₂ is in a high-resistance state. When the target gas reacts, electrons are released, conductance rises, Rs falls, and the load-resistor output voltage shifts — a measurable analog signal.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Distance Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[MCU Sends 10 us Pulse] --> nodeB[TRIG Pin]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Driver Circuit Activates]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Piezo TX Emits 8 pulses of 40 kHz]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Sound Travels Through Air at v]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Object Reflects Echo]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Piezo RX Detects Echo]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Amplifier Circuit]
    nodeH --> nodeI[ECHO Pin Held HIGH for Time t]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[MCU Computes d equals v t over 2]
    style nodeD fill:#ADD8E6
    style nodeG fill:#FFA07A
    style nodeJ fill:#90EE90
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The trigger pulse initiates an 8-cycle ultrasonic burst. After reflection, the receiver activates the ECHO pin for a duration equal to the round-trip travel time. The MCU uses this pulse width to compute distance via the time-of-flight formula.

---

## 4.4 Pinout Reference Diagram (HC-SR04 Physical Interface)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pin1[VCC 5V] --> pinA[Pin 1]
    pin2[TRIG Input] --> pinB[Pin 2]
    pin3[ECHO Output] --> pinC[Pin 3]
    pin4[GND] --> pinD[Pin 4]
    pinA --> hcSR04[HC SR04 Module]
    pinB --> hcSR04
    pinC --> hcSR04
    pinD --> hcSR04
    style pin1 fill:#FFB6C1
    style pin2 fill:#FFD700
    style pin3 fill:#90EE90
    style pin4 fill:#D3D3D3
    style hcSR04 fill:#87CEEB
```

---

## 4.5 Timing Diagram Description (HC-SR04 — for understanding only)

1. **Trigger Sequence:** MCU pulls TRIG HIGH for at least 10 µs, then LOW.
2. **Burst Phase:** Internally, 8 cycles of 40 kHz are emitted (~200 µs total).
3. **Echo Window:** ECHO pin goes HIGH; the rising edge starts $t$.
4. **Echo Falling Edge:** When the receiver detects the reflected burst, ECHO goes LOW; the falling edge ends $t$.
5. **Computation:** MCU reads $t$ using a hardware timer or `pulseIn()`-style function.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners may ask for a **timing diagram** of HC-SR04 in descriptive form. Always label: TRIG pulse, 8-burst emission, ECHO HIGH pulse width = $t$, and the derived distance value.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP, Set A]`
**Q: With a neat diagram, explain the working principle of the DHT11 temperature and humidity sensor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The DHT11 contains two sensing elements:
1. **Humidity sensor:** A polymer capacitive element whose dielectric constant changes with relative humidity. The change in capacitance is converted into a digital RH value by an on-board ASIC.
2. **Temperature sensor:** An NTC thermistor whose resistance decreases as temperature rises (Negative Temperature Coefficient). The Steinhart-Hart equation governs this relationship: $\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{T_0} + \frac{1}{\beta} \ln(\frac{R}{R_0})$.

Both values are transmitted to the microcontroller via a single-wire bidirectional protocol as a **40-bit packet** (16-bit RH + 16-bit T + 8-bit checksum). The sensor requires 3 – 5 V supply and a 10 kΩ pull-up resistor on the data line.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP, Set B]`
**Q: Briefly describe the operating principle of an MQ-series gas sensor. What is the role of the heater coil?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The MQ-series gas sensor uses a **tin dioxide (SnO₂) semiconductor layer** whose electrical resistance changes when exposed to specific gases. The internal heater coil (typically 5 V, ~800 mW) heats the SnO₂ to **200 – 400 °C**, which is essential for the chemi-resistive reaction.

In clean air, oxygen adsorbs on the heated SnO₂ surface and traps conduction electrons, leaving the sensor in a **high-resistance state**. When a target gas (e.g., LPG) reacts with the adsorbed oxygen, the trapped electrons are released, **lowering the resistance**. The change in resistance, measured via an external load resistor, is proportional to gas concentration. The heater must remain powered continuously for stable operation and requires a **24–48 hour burn-in** at first use.

---

## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Rule:** Answer ANY ONE full question from each module. Each long-answer carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** With a suitable block diagram, explain the **operating principle of the DHT22 temperature and humidity sensor**. Compare its features with the DHT11. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expression for the **speed-of-sound correction** in air and explain how the **HC-SR04** uses this to compute distance. A sensor returns an echo time of **1176 µs** at 25 °C. Calculate the distance to the object. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

1. **Definition [1 mark]:** The DHT22 is a digital-output, factory-calibrated combined temperature and relative humidity sensor that uses capacitive and resistive sensing elements.
2. **Humidity element [2 marks]:** A hydrophilic polymer film is sandwiched between two electrodes. The polymer absorbs water vapor; the dielectric constant $\varepsilon_r$ changes, modifying the capacitance $C_h = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$. An onboard ASIC digitizes this change.
3. **Temperature element [2 marks]:** An NTC thermistor (typically 10 kΩ at 25 °C, $\beta = 3950$) changes resistance per $R = R_0 \cdot \exp[\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)]$. The voltage across it in a divider is digitized.
4. **Comparison table [2 marks]:** DHT22 offers 0–100% RH range, ±2% accuracy, -40 to 80 °C range, 0.1 °C resolution vs DHT11's 20–90% RH, ±5%, 0–50 °C, 1 °C resolution. DHT11 is cheaper, DHT22 more precise.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

1. **Speed of sound equation [2 marks]:** $v = 331.3 + 0.606 T$ (m/s), where T is in °C.
2. **Time-of-flight equation [1 mark]:** $d = v \cdot t / 2$ (the factor 2 for round-trip).
3. **Plug in T = 25 °C [1 mark]:** $v = 331.3 + 0.606 \times 25 = 331.3 + 15.15 = 346.45$ m/s.
4. **Convert time [1 mark]:** $t = 1176 \times 10^{-6}$ s.
5. **Compute distance [2 marks]:**
    - Round-trip distance: $v \cdot t = 346.45 \times 1176 \times 10^{-6} \approx 0.4074$ m
    - One-way distance: $d = 0.4074 / 2 \approx 0.2037$ m $\approx$ **20.4 cm**

> **Final Answer:** The object is **approximately 20.4 cm** from the HC-SR04 sensor.

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of the MQ-2 gas sensor** with a labeled block diagram. Discuss the role of oxygen adsorption and the heater coil. Why is a load resistor used in the output circuit? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An NTC thermistor inside a DHT22 has $R_0 = 10$ kΩ at 25 °C and $\beta = 3950$ K. Calculate its resistance at **60 °C**. Also explain why the SnO₂ layer must be heated for the MQ sensor to function. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]

1. **Architecture [1 mark]:** MQ-2 has a ceramic substrate with a SnO₂ sensing layer and a Ni-Cr heater coil. Two electrodes measure resistance.
2. **Clean-air state [1.5 marks]:** Heated SnO₂ surface adsorbs atmospheric O₂; electrons are trapped, forming a depletion layer; resistance $R_s$ is high.
3. **Gas-present state [1.5 marks]:** Reducing gas (LPG/H₂/CO) reacts with adsorbed O₂, releases electrons, lowers $R_s$ (resistance drops, conductance rises).
4. **Role of heater [1 mark]:** Provides activation energy for surface reactions; ensures selectivity and stable sensitivity; must be on continuously.
5. **Load resistor role [1 mark]:** A load resistor $R_L$ in series forms a voltage divider. The output voltage $V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot R_L / (R_s + R_L)$ converts the resistance change into a measurable voltage that the ADC can read.
6. **Block diagram [1 mark]:** As shown in SECTION 4.2 (mermaid block diagram of MQ series).

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks] — **second subpart**

Wait — Part B(b) below.

#### ✅ Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]

1. **Convert temperature to Kelvin [1 mark]:** $T_0 = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15$ K; $T = 60 + 273.15 = 333.15$ K.
2. **Apply NTC formula [1 mark]:** $R = R_0 \cdot \exp[\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)]$.
3. **Compute the bracket [1 mark]:**
    - $1/T = 1/333.15 \approx 3.0017 \times 10^{-3}$ K⁻¹
    - $1/T_0 = 1/298.15 \approx 3.3540 \times 10^{-3}$ K⁻¹
    - Difference: $-3.523 \times 10^{-4}$ K⁻¹
4. **Multiply by $\beta$ [1 mark]:** $3950 \times (-3.523 \times 10^{-4}) \approx -1.3916$.
5. **Exponentiate [1 mark]:** $\exp(-1.3916) \approx 0.2486$.
6. **Compute final resistance [1 mark]:** $R = 10 \text{ k}\Omega \times 0.2486 \approx 2.49$ kΩ.
7. **Why heat SnO₂ [1 mark]:** At room temperature, the surface reactions are too slow; heating provides activation energy so oxygen adsorption and gas-surface redox reactions occur reliably and reproducibly, giving consistent sensitivity.

> **Final Answer:** $R \approx$ **2.49 kΩ at 60 °C**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the ÷2 in HC-SR04 distance:** The echo time is for *round-trip*. Writing $d = v \cdot t$ (without ÷2) costs **2 marks** immediately. KTU key-checkers look for this specifically.
> 2. **Mixing up DHT11 and DHT22 specs:** Saying DHT11 has 0–100% RH range is wrong — it's 20–90%. Marks are deducted for swapped values.
> 3. **MQ sensor needs heater:** Many students write that "MQ works at room temperature." It does not. The heater coil is **mandatory** for sensor operation.
> 4. **Steinhart-Hart equation confusion:** The β-equation is *not* a linear relationship. Don't write $R = R_0(1 + \beta(T - T_0))$; that is a **metal** thermistor approximation, not NTC.
> 5. **Speed of sound in vacuum:** $v$ is meaningless in vacuum. Always state that HC-SR04 works in air, and the value $v \approx 343$ m/s applies only at standard conditions.
> 6. **Units:** Always include units (Ω, kΩ, m, cm, m/s, °C, K). Missing units lose half-marks across the paper.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2 (Commercial Sensors)**

- ☐ **DHT11 / DHT22** are **digital** sensors using a single-wire bus. They sense humidity via a **capacitive polymer** and temperature via an **NTC thermistor**.
- ☐ The NTC thermistor equation: $R = R_0 \cdot \exp[\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)]$. Typical $\beta = 3950$, $R_0 = 10$ kΩ at 25 °C.
- ☐ DHT11 = cheap, lower accuracy; DHT22 = higher accuracy, wider range.
- ☐ **MQ Series** are **chemi-resistive** sensors using a **SnO₂ layer** heated to 200–400 °C. Resistance falls when reducing gases are present.
- ☐ A **load resistor $R_L$** converts the resistance change to a voltage: $V_{out} = V_{cc} \cdot R_L / (R_s + R_L)$.
- ☐ MQ sensors require a **24–48 hour burn-in** before stable readings are obtained.
- ☐ **HC-SR04** uses **ultrasonic time-of-flight**. Trigger with a 10 µs pulse; receive an 8-cycle 40 kHz burst.
- ☐ Distance formula: $d = v \cdot t / 2$, where $v = 331.3 + 0.606 T$ (m/s) at temperature $T$ in °C.
- ☐ HC-SR04 range: **2 cm – 400 cm**; beam cone ~15°; cannot detect very soft or steeply angled objects.
- ☐ All three sensors are part of the **Perception Layer** of the IoT stack and feed data to the **Network/Processing Layer** via microcontrollers (e.g., Arduino, ESP32, Raspberry Pi Pico).
- ☐ **Common exam trick:** Always mention **temperature compensation** in HC-SR04 (speed of sound changes with T) and **heater warm-up time** for MQ sensors.
- ☐ KTU 2024 expects **operating principles only** — focus on *how* the sensor works physically/chemically/acoustically, not on the programming API.

---

**End of Module 2 Topic Notes — Commercially Available Sensors (DHT11/22, MQ Series, HC-SR04)**
*Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme, OEAET721 — Internet of Things.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Actuators (Working Principles Only) - DC Motors, Solenoid Based Valves, Electro-Mechanical Relay, Solid State Relay, Stepper Motors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEAET721 - INTERNET OF THINGS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Actuators (Working Principles Only) - DC Motors, Solenoid Based Valves, Electro-Mechanical Relay, Solid State Relay, Stepper Motors ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is an Actuator? (KTU Definition) An **Actuator** is a physical output device that converts an electrical control signal (typically low-power, from a microcontroller, IoT node, or driver circuit) into a **physical action** such as motion, rotation, linear displacement, heat, light, or fluid/gas flow. In the IoT framework, actuators are the **"muscles"** of the system — they perform the actual work in the real world after sensors (the "senses") gather data and the processing unit (the "brain") makes a decision. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** This module requires the **Working Principle ONLY** of each actuator. Numerical design, controller tuning, and detailed electromagnetic derivations are **out of scope** for this module. Focus strictly on the energy-conversion mechanism and the input–output relationship. ### 1.2 The Four Key Characteristics of Every Actuator | Characteristic | Meaning | Typical Unit | |---|---|---| | **Input Signal** | Electrical control from the IoT node | Volts (V), Amps (A), PWM Duty (%) | | **Output Action** | Physical phenomenon produced | Rotation (RPM), Force (N), Stroke (mm) | | **Driver Required?** | Buffer between logic-level MCU and actuator | Yes / No (with details) | | **Response Time** | Speed at which it reacts to the input | ms / s | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body 🦾 > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** Think of a smart home system. Your brain (microcontroller) decides "the room is too hot." Your skin (temperature sensor) reports this. Your hand then walks to the AC and switches it on. **The hand is the actuator** — it converts the brain's electrical "decision" into physical movement. In IoT, an actuator does the same: it converts an electrical signal from a chip (e.g., ESP32, Arduino, Raspberry Pi) into a real-world mechanical or thermal action. ### 1.4 The Five Actuators in Focus — At a Glance - **DC Motor** → Continuous rotation (spinning shaft) for fans, wheels, pumps, drones. - **Solenoid-Based Valve** → Linear push/pull motion to open/close fluid or gas pathways. - **Electromechanical (EM) Relay** → Electromagnetic switch for high-power circuit ON/OFF. - **Solid State Relay (SSR)** → Semiconductor-based silent switch for the same ON/OFF function. - **Stepper Motor** → Precise, discrete angular stepping motion for 3D printers, CNC, robotics. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 DC Motor — Working Principle A **DC (Direct Current) Motor** converts DC electrical energy into continuous rotational mechanical energy using the interaction of magnetic fields. **Operating Logic (Step-by-Step):** 1. A **permanent magnet** (stator) creates a steady magnetic field between the north and south poles. 2. A **rotating coil (armature)** placed inside this field carries DC current supplied via brushes and a commutator. 3. According to the **Fleming Left-Hand Rule**, the current-carrying conductor experiences a force, producing a **torque**. 4. The **commutator** (a split-ring) reverses the current direction every half-rotation, ensuring the torque always acts in the **same rotational direction** — producing continuous rotation. 5. Speed is controlled by varying the **applied voltage** or using **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Direction of Rotation:** Determined by Fleming's Left-Hand Rule. Reverse either the magnetic field polarity OR the current direction → the motor spins the opposite way. **DC Motor Formula Sheet (KTU 2024):** | Parameter | Formula | Description | |---|---|---| | Back-EMF | $E_b = K \cdot \Phi \cdot N$ | $K$ = machine constant, $\Phi$ = flux, $N$ = speed | | Voltage Equation | $V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a$ | $V$ = supply, $I_a$ = armature current, $R_a$ = armature resistance | | Torque | $T = K \cdot \Phi \cdot I_a$ | Torque ∝ armature current | | Speed (no-load) | $N \propto \dfrac{V}{\Phi}$ | Speed varies directly with voltage | --- ### 2.2 Solenoid-Based Valve — Working Principle A **Solenoid Valve** is an electromechanically operated valve used to control the flow of liquids or gases (water, air, oil, refrigerants). **Operating Logic:** 1. The device contains a **solenoid** — a coil of wire wound around a movable ferromagnetic **plunger (core)**. 2. When the coil is **energized** (current flows), it generates a **magnetic field** that pulls the plunger toward the center of the coil. 3. The plunger's movement **opens or closes a valve seat**, allowing or blocking the fluid pathway. 4. When the coil is **de-energized**, a **spring** pushes the plunger back to its original (rest) position. 5. Solenoid valves are categorized as **Normally Closed (NC)** or **Normally Open (NO)**. > [!NOTE] > **Two Types to Remember:** (1) **Direct-acting** — plunger directly opens/closes the orifice. (2) **Pilot-operated** — plunger only opens a small pilot hole, and the main flow is driven by fluid pressure. **Solenoid Force Formula:** $$F = \frac{\mu_0 \cdot N^2 \cdot I^2 \cdot A}{2 \cdot g^2}$$ Where $F$ = force on plunger, $N$ = number of turns, $I$ = coil current, $A$ = cross-sectional area, $g$ = air-gap length. --- ### 2.3 Electromechanical (EM) Relay — Working Principle An **EM Relay** is an electrically controlled **mechanical switch** that uses an electromagnet to open or close one or more sets of contacts. **Operating Logic:** 1. The relay has two electrically isolated circuits: the **primary (coil) circuit** and the **secondary (contact) circuit**. 2. When a small current flows through the **coil**, it produces a magnetic field that attracts a movable **armature**. 3. The armature's movement causes the **common (COM) contact** to either connect to the **Normally Open (NO)** contact or the **Normally Closed (NC)** contact. 4. When the coil is de-energized, a **spring** returns the armature to its rest state. 5. This allows a **low-power MCU signal (e.g., 5 V, 20 mA)** to control a **high-power load (e.g., 230 V AC, 10 A)** with **complete galvanic isolation**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Point:** The relay provides **galvanic isolation** — there is NO electrical connection between the control circuit and the load circuit. This is its biggest safety advantage. --- ### 2.4 Solid State Relay (SSR) — Working Principle A **Solid State Relay (SSR)** performs the same ON/OFF switching function as an EM relay, but uses **semiconductor devices** instead of moving parts. **Operating Logic:** 1. The input side uses an **opto-coupler (LED + phototransistor)** that converts the control signal into light. 2. The light activates a semiconductor switching element on the output side — typically a **TRIAC** (for AC loads) or a **MOSFET / IGBT** (for DC loads). 3. When the control signal is ON → the semiconductor conducts → load is switched ON. 4. When the control signal is OFF → the semiconductor blocks → load is switched OFF. 5. Zero-crossing detection circuits in the TRIAC ensure the relay switches only when the AC mains voltage is at 0 V → reduces electrical noise and EMI. > [!NOTE] > **Why SSRs Exist:** They have **no moving parts**, hence no contact wear, no arcing, no bounce, and switching speeds in **microseconds** versus **milliseconds** for EM relays. --- ### 2.5 Stepper Motor — Working Principle A **Stepper Motor** is a brushless DC motor that divides a full rotation into a **discrete number of equal steps**. Each electrical pulse moves the shaft by one fixed angular increment. **Operating Logic:** 1. The motor has a **stator** with multiple **electromagnetic coils (phases)** and a **rotor** made of permanent magnets or a toothed iron core. 2. Energizing the coils in a **specific sequence** attracts the rotor teeth to align with the energized stator poles. 3. Switching the energization to the next coil in the sequence advances the rotor by exactly **one step angle** ($\alpha$). 4. The sequence can be **full-step, half-step, or microstep**, giving different levels of resolution and smoothness. 5. The rotor's final position is **retained by the magnetic detent torque** even when power is removed (open-loop position control). **Step Angle Formula:** $$\alpha = \frac{360^\circ}{N \cdot m}$$ Where $N$ = number of stator teeth, $m$ = number of phases. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stepper vs DC Motor — Examiner's Favourite Comparison:** A stepper motor gives **precise positional control without feedback** (open-loop). A DC motor gives **smooth continuous rotation** but needs an encoder for position control (closed-loop). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Back-EMF and Speed Equation of a DC Motor We derive the relationship between **supply voltage, speed, and flux** — a high-yield KTU topic. **Step 1: Define the voltage across the armature circuit** The supply voltage $V$ drives the armature current $I_a$ through the armature winding, but a portion of it is opposed by the **back-EMF** $E_b$ generated by the rotating coil (Faraday's Law): $$V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a$$ **Step 2: Express the back-EMF in terms of speed** By Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction, the EMF induced in a rotating armature is directly proportional to the **flux per pole** $\Phi$ and the **angular speed** $N$: $$E_b = K \cdot \Phi \cdot N$$ Where $K$ is the machine's construction constant. **Step 3: Substitute and isolate speed** Substituting the back-EMF expression into the voltage equation: $$V = K \cdot \Phi \cdot N + I_a \cdot R_a$$ Solving for $N$: $$N = \frac{V - I_a \cdot R_a}{K \cdot \Phi}$$ **Step 4: Apply the no-load condition** At no-load, the armature current $I_a$ is negligibly small (only the field current), so $I_a \cdot R_a \approx 0$: $$N_{no\text{-}load} = \frac{V}{K \cdot \Phi}$$ **Conclusion:** The no-load speed of a DC motor is **directly proportional** to the supply voltage $V$ and **inversely proportional** to the field flux $\Phi$. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Step Angle of a Stepper Motor **Step 1: Understand the geometry** A stepper motor with $N$ teeth on the rotor stator surface and $m$ phases energizes in a cyclic sequence. Each step advances the rotor by one tooth-pitch. **Step 2: Express angular tooth pitch** The angular distance between two adjacent teeth (one step): $$\alpha = \frac{360^\circ}{N}$$ **Step 3: Account for number of phases** If $m$ phases are excited in sequence, the effective stepping for a given energization cycle becomes: $$\alpha_{step} = \frac{360^\circ}{N \cdot m}$$ **Numerical Example:** For a stepper motor with $N = 50$ teeth and $m = 2$ phases: $$\alpha_{step} = \frac{360^\circ}{50 \times 2} = \frac{360^\circ}{100} = 3.6^\circ \text{ per step}$$ For $N = 200$ teeth and $m = 4$ phases: $$\alpha_{step} = \frac{360^\circ}{200 \times 4} = 0.45^\circ \text{ per step}$$ This is the famous **1.8° per step** resolution found in many industrial motors. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: EM Relay vs Solid State Relay (SSR) | Comparison Parameter | Electromechanical (EM) Relay | Solid State Relay (SSR) | |---|---|---| | **Switching Element** | Movable metal contacts | TRIAC / MOSFET / IGBT | | **Isolation Method** | Magnetic (coil) | Optical (opto-coupler) | | **Switching Speed** | 5–15 ms (slow) | < 1 µs (fast) | | **Contact Wear** | Yes — limited mechanical life | None — no moving parts | | **Electrical Noise** | Clicking + contact bounce | Silent, but minor leakage current | | **Load Type** | AC or DC | Usually AC (TRIAC) or DC (MOSFET) | | **Power Loss** | Very low (mΩ contact) | Higher (heat sink often needed) | | **Cost** | Low | Higher | | **Typical Use** | Low-frequency switching, automotive | High-frequency switching, PLC, IoT | --- ### 3.4 Comparative Summary of All Five Actuators | Actuator | Input | Output | Movement Type | Typical IoT Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | **DC Motor** | DC voltage / PWM | Rotation | Continuous | Smart fan, robot wheels, water pump | | **Solenoid Valve** | DC voltage ON/OFF | Linear push/pull | Discrete (Open/Close) | Automatic irrigation, gas cutoff | | **EM Relay** | Low DC voltage | Switch ON/OFF | Discrete contact | Mains appliance control, motor starters | | **SSR** | Low DC voltage | Switch ON/OFF | Solid-state | Industrial heater, PLC outputs | | **Stepper Motor** | Sequence of pulses | Rotation (discrete) | Step-by-step | 3D printer, camera gimbal, CNC plotter | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 DC Motor — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[IoT Microcontroller
e.g. ESP32] -->|PWM Signal| B[Motor Driver
L298N / DRV8871] B -->|DC Voltage| C[DC Motor Stator
Permanent Magnets] C --> D[Armature Rotor
Coil + Commutator] D -->|Rotating Shaft| E[Mechanical Load
Fan / Wheel / Pump] D -->|Back EMF Feedback| B style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333 style B fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333 style C fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333 style D fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333 style E fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333 ``` ### 4.2 Solenoid-Based Valve — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB MCU[IoT Node
Digital Output] -->|Driver Transistor| COIL[Solenoid Coil
Wound Around Plunger] COIL -->|Magnetic Field| PLUNGER[Ferromagnetic Plunger] PLUNGER -->|Linear Motion| VALVE[Valve Seat
Open or Close] VALVE --> FLOW[Fluid Path
Water or Gas] SPRING[Return Spring] -.->|Restores Position| PLUNGER style MCU fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333 style COIL fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333 style PLUNGER fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333 style VALVE fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333 style FLOW fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#333 style SPRING fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#333 ``` ### 4.3 Electromechanical Relay — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR CTRL[MCU Control Signal
5V 20mA] --> COIL1[Primary Coil
Electromagnet] COIL1 -->|Magnetic Flux| ARM[Movable Armature] ARM -->|Mechanical Pull| COM[Common Contact] COM --> NO[Normally Open Contact] COM -.-> NC[Normally Closed Contact] NO --> LOAD[High Power Load
230V AC Lamp] SPRING2[Spring] -.->|Restores| ARM style CTRL fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333 style COIL1 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333 style ARM fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333 style COM fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333 style NO fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333 style NC fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333 style LOAD fill:#f4d03f,stroke:#333 style SPRING2 fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#333 ``` ### 4.4 Solid State Relay — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR IN[Input Signal
3-32V DC] --> LED[Infrared LED
in Optocoupler] LED -->|Light Pulses| PHOTO[Phototransistor
Isolated Barrier] PHOTO --> TRIAC[TRIAC Output
or MOSFET] TRIAC -->|Switches| ACLOAD[AC Mains Load
Heater or Motor] ZC[Zero Crossing
Detector] -.->|Synchronizes| TRIAC style IN fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333 style LED fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333 style PHOTO fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333 style TRIAC fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333 style ACLOAD fill:#f4d03f,stroke:#333 style ZC fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#333 ``` ### 4.5 Stepper Motor — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB SEQ[Step Sequence Generator
MCU or Driver IC] -->|Pulse 1| P1[Phase A Coil ON] SEQ -->|Pulse 2| P2[Phase B Coil ON] SEQ -->|Pulse 3| P3[Phase A Coil OFF] SEQ -->|Pulse 4| P4[Phase B Coil OFF] P1 --> ROTOR[Rotor Aligned with Pole A] P2 --> ROTOR2[Rotor Aligned with Pole B] P3 --> ROTOR3[Rotor Advances 1 Step] P4 --> ROTOR4[Full Cycle Complete] ROTOR4 -->|Continuous Pulses| SHAFT[Stepped Shaft Rotation] style SEQ fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333 style P1 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333 style P2 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333 style P3 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333 style P4 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333 style SHAFT fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333 ``` ### 4.6 Unified IoT Actuator Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR SENSOR[Sensor Layer
Temperature, Humidity, PIR] --> MCU[IoT Microcontroller
Decision Logic] MCU -->|GPIO PWM| DRV[Driver / Isolation Stage] DRV --> ACT[Actuator Bank
DC Motor, Relay, SSR,
Solenoid, Stepper] ACT --> WORLD[Physical World Action
Spin, Switch, Open, Step] CLOUD[Cloud Dashboard] -.->|Command| MCU style SENSOR fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333 style MCU fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333 style DRV fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333 style ACT fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333 style WORLD fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#333 style CLOUD fill:#d7bde2,stroke:#333 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023 Style]** *With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a DC motor. State Fleming's Left-Hand Rule.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A DC motor converts electrical energy into mechanical rotation using the interaction between a magnetic field and a current-carrying conductor. **Working Principle (2 marks):** 1. A permanent magnet (stator) produces a uniform magnetic field between its north and south poles. 2. A current-carrying armature coil is placed inside this field. 3. According to Fleming's Left-Hand Rule, a force $F = BIL$ acts on the conductor, producing a torque. 4. The commutator reverses the current direction every half-turn, ensuring unidirectional rotation. 5. The shaft rotates continuously and drives the mechanical load. **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule (1 mark):** Hold the left hand with thumb, forefinger, and middle finger mutually perpendicular — **Forefinger** = Field, **Middle** = Current, **Thumb** = Motion (Force). --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Style]** *Compare Electromechanical (EM) Relay and Solid State Relay (SSR) on the basis of (i) switching element, (ii) speed, and (iii) isolation method.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | EM Relay | SSR | |---|---|---| | (i) Switching Element | Movable metal contacts (mechanical) | TRIAC / MOSFET (semiconductor) | | (ii) Switching Speed | 5–15 ms (slow) | < 1 µs (very fast) | | (iii) Isolation Method | Magnetic (electromagnetic coil) | Optical (opto-coupler) | **Conclusion (extra line for full marks):** SSRs offer silent, fast, contactless switching but generate more heat, while EM relays handle higher inrush currents and provide true galvanic isolation. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks): DC Motor + Stepper Motor** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]** * **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a DC motor. Derive the relationship between supply voltage, back-EMF, and speed. **(7 marks)** * **(b)** Explain the working principle of a stepper motor. Derive the step angle formula and compute the step angle for $N = 200$ teeth, $m = 4$ phases. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) DC Motor — Working Principle + Derivation (7 marks):** **Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.1. **Working Principle (3 marks):** A DC motor consists of a stator (permanent magnet or field winding) and a rotor (armature with commutator). When DC supply is given to the armature, current flows through the coil. According to **Fleming's Left-Hand Rule**, a force acts on the conductor: $$F = B \cdot I \cdot L$$ This produces a torque on the armature. The **commutator** reverses the current every half-rotation, ensuring continuous unidirectional motion. The motor thus converts DC electrical energy into mechanical rotational energy. **Derivation (2 marks):** The voltage across the armature circuit is: $$V = E_b + I_a \cdot R_a$$ Where $E_b = K \cdot \Phi \cdot N$ is the back-EMF. Substituting: $$V = K \cdot \Phi \cdot N + I_a \cdot R_a$$ Rearranging for speed: $$N = \frac{V - I_a \cdot R_a}{K \cdot \Phi}$$ At no-load, $I_a \cdot R_a \approx 0$, so: $$N_{no\text{-}load} = \frac{V}{K \cdot \Phi}$$ **Conclusion:** Speed is directly proportional to supply voltage $V$ and inversely proportional to flux $\Phi$. --- **(b) Stepper Motor — Working Principle + Step Angle (7 marks):** **Working Principle (3 marks):** A stepper motor has a **stator** with multiple phase windings and a **rotor** of permanent magnets or toothed iron. When the phase windings are energized in a **specific sequence**, the rotor aligns itself with the energized stator pole, moving by one **step angle**. Each input pulse causes one step, making the rotation **discrete and precise** without needing feedback (open-loop control). **Modes of Operation (1 mark):** - Full-step - Half-step - Microstepping **Derivation of Step Angle (2 marks):** The angular distance between two adjacent stator teeth is: $$\alpha_{tooth} = \frac{360^\circ}{N}$$ For $m$ phases energizing in cyclic sequence, the effective step per pulse is: $$\alpha_{step} = \frac{360^\circ}{N \cdot m}$$ **Numerical Computation (1 mark):** For $N = 200$, $m = 4$: $$\alpha_{step} = \frac{360^\circ}{200 \times 4} = \frac{360^\circ}{800} = 0.45^\circ \text{ per step}$$ This is the standard **1.8° / 0.45°** resolution family of industrial stepper motors. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks): Solenoid + SSR** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Style]** * **(a)** Explain the working principle of a solenoid-based valve. List its two main types and one industrial IoT application. **(7 marks)** * **(b)** With a block diagram, explain the working principle of a Solid State Relay. Compare it with an Electromechanical Relay in 4 points. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Solenoid-Based Valve (7 marks):** **Working Principle (4 marks):** A solenoid valve uses an electromagnetic coil to control fluid flow. The structure includes: - A **solenoid coil** wound around a hollow former. - A movable **ferromagnetic plunger (core)**. - A **valve seat** and a **return spring**. When current flows through the coil, a magnetic field is produced (per **Ampere's Law**), which pulls the plunger inside the coil against the spring force. This movement either **opens or closes** the valve seat, allowing or blocking the flow of fluid (water, oil, gas, air). When the coil is de-energized, the spring pushes the plunger back to its original position, restoring the default flow state. **Types (2 marks):** 1. **Direct-Acting Solenoid Valve** — Plunger directly opens/closes the orifice. Works without fluid pressure differential. 2. **Pilot-Operated Solenoid Valve** — Plunger opens a small pilot hole, and the main flow is driven by the line pressure. Used for high-flow applications. **IoT Application (1 mark):** - **Smart Irrigation Systems** — ESP32 detects soil moisture, controls a 12 V solenoid valve to release water to crops automatically. --- **(b) Solid State Relay (7 marks):** **Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.4. **Working Principle (3 marks):** A Solid State Relay uses semiconductor devices to perform switching without any moving parts. 1. The input control signal (3–32 V DC) is applied to an **infrared LED** inside an **opto-coupler**. 2. The LED emits light that falls on a **phototransistor** on the output side, providing **galvanic isolation** between input and output. 3. The phototransistor triggers a semiconductor switch — a **TRIAC** (for AC loads) or **MOSFET/IGBT** (for DC loads). 4. When triggered, the semiconductor conducts, completing the load circuit. 5. A **zero-crossing detector** ensures switching occurs only when the AC voltage is at 0 V, reducing EMI. **Comparison with EM Relay (2 marks):** | Parameter | EM Relay | SSR | |---|---|---| | Switching Element | Movable metal contacts | Semiconductor (TRIAC/MOSFET) | | Switching Speed | 5–15 ms | < 1 µs | | Lifetime | Limited (mechanical wear) | Very long (no moving parts) | | Noise | Audible click, contact bounce | Silent, but may have leakage current | --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Lose Marks Here!** > - **Forgetting the commutation role:** Many students write about DC motor working but **forget to mention the commutator** — this costs a full mark. Always explain that the commutator reverses the current every half-rotation. > - **Mixing up SSR terminology:** Students often write "SSR uses a relay coil" — **WRONG**. SSR has **no coil and no contacts**. Use the word "TRIAC" or "semiconductor switch". > - **Step angle formula units:** Writing 360/N without accounting for phases $m$ is a common mistake. Full credit only if the formula includes $m$. > - **Solenoid valve type confusion:** Don't call a solenoid valve a "solenoid" alone. It's a **valve** controlled by a solenoid. > - **Relay contact nomenclature:** Always label COM, NO, and NC in the diagram — vague diagrams lose 1 mark. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️ > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 Exam** - ✅ **DC Motor:** Permanent magnet stator + current-carrying armature + commutator. Speed ∝ $V/\Phi$. Direction = Fleming's Left-Hand Rule. - ✅ **Solenoid Valve:** Coil energizes → plunger moves → valve opens/closes. Two types: **Direct-acting** and **Pilot-operated**. - ✅ **EM Relay:** Electromagnetic coil attracts armature → COM switches between NO and NC. Provides **galvanic isolation**. - ✅ **SSR:** Opto-coupler + TRIAC/MOSFET. **No moving parts.** Zero-crossing switching for AC loads. Fast, silent, long life. - ✅ **Stepper Motor:** Pulse-driven discrete rotation. Step angle = $360° / (N \cdot m)$. Standard industrial step: **1.8° (200 teeth, 2 phases)** or **0.45°**. - ✅ **IoT Block:** Sensor → MCU → Driver → Actuator → Physical Action. - ✅ **Comparison Favourite:** EM Relay vs SSR is a guaranteed question — memorize the 4-parameter table. - ✅ **Application Match:** DC Motor = fan/wheel. Solenoid = water valve. Relay = mains switch. SSR = heater/PLC. Stepper = 3D printer. - ✅ **Key Formulas:** $V = E_b + I_a R_a$, $N = V/(K\Phi)$, $F = \mu_0 N^2 I^2 A / (2g^2)$, $\alpha_{step} = 360°/(Nm)$.

topics.title: Embedded Boards - Overview of Typical Microcontroller and Single Board Computers Used in IoT Applications - Arduino Uno, ESP32, Raspberry Pi (Overview Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET721 - INTERNET OF THINGS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Embedded Boards - Overview of Typical Microcontroller and Single Board Computers Used in IoT Applications - Arduino Uno, ESP32, Raspberry Pi (Overview Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Embedded Boards in IoT?

**Embedded boards** are compact, self-contained computing platforms that integrate a processor, memory, and input/output (I/O) peripherals on a single printed circuit board (PCB). In the context of **Internet of Things (IoT)**, these boards act as the physical edge devices that sense, process, and communicate data from the real world to the network/cloud.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> "Embedded boards are the cornerstone hardware platforms of IoT systems. They can be broadly classified into two categories:
> **(a) Microcontroller Boards** — e.g., Arduino Uno, ESP32 (resource-constrained, deterministic, real-time)
> **(b) Single Board Computers (SBCs)** — e.g., Raspberry Pi (full-fledged OS-running computational platforms)"

## 1.2 Microcontroller vs. Single Board Computer — The Foundational Distinction

| Parameter | Microcontroller Board | Single Board Computer (SBC) |
|---|---|---|
| Processing Core | MCU (single chip, CPU + RAM + ROM) | Full Application Processor (AP) |
| Operating System | None / RTOS (Real-Time OS) | Full OS (Linux, Raspbian, etc.) |
| Power Consumption | **mW range (0.1 W – 1 W)** | **W range (2 W – 7 W)** |
| Clock Speed | **8 MHz – 240 MHz** | **700 MHz – 1.8 GHz** |
| Cost | **₹200 – ₹600** | **₹3,000 – ₹8,000** |
| Real-Time Response | Deterministic (microsecond latency) | Non-deterministic (OS jitter) |
| Examples | Arduino Uno, ESP32, NodeMCU | Raspberry Pi 4, BeagleBone |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Three Workers in a Smart Home

Imagine a **smart home with three workers**, each with different roles:

- **Arduino Uno is the Electrician** 🛠️ — A skilled, low-cost worker who does one specific job extremely reliably: reading a temperature sensor, blinking an LED, or opening a relay. He does not get distracted, has no fancy office, and responds in **microseconds**. He is perfect for **real-time actuator control**.

- **ESP32 is the Wireless Security Guard** 📡 — The same kind of reliable worker as the electrician, BUT he comes with a built-in **walkie-talkie (Wi-Fi + Bluetooth)** and can patrol the entire neighborhood. He is the **best choice when you need cloud connectivity on a budget**.

- **Raspberry Pi is the Building Manager** 🖥️ — A highly educated, full-time manager with a **personal office, computer, and personal assistant (Linux OS)**. He can run multiple tasks simultaneously, watch camera feeds, host a webpage, and even make decisions using **AI/ML models**. However, he consumes more power and salary (cost).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for KTU Exams:** Whenever a question gives an IoT scenario, your first step is to classify it as:
> 1. **Real-time sensing/control?** → Microcontroller (Arduino/ESP32)
> 2. **Heavy compute + OS + ML + Multimedia?** → SBC (Raspberry Pi)
> 3. **Wireless + Cloud on budget?** → ESP32 is the sweet spot.

## 1.4 The IoT Stack — Where Embedded Boards Fit

Embedded boards occupy **Layer 1 (Perception) and Layer 2 (Edge/Network) of the 5-layer IoT architecture**, sitting between the physical sensors/actuators and the network gateway/cloud.

The five layers are:
1. **Perception / Sensor Layer** — Sensors + Embedded Board (e.g., DHT22 + ESP32)
2. **Network / Edge Layer** — Communication (Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa, MQTT)
3. **Middleware / Processing Layer** — Edge gateways (often a Raspberry Pi)
4. **Application Layer** — User-facing apps, dashboards
5. **Business Layer** — Analytics, decision-making

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for KTU:** *"Arduino senses, ESP32 senses-and-sends, Raspberry Pi thinks-and-decides."*
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Arduino Uno — The Microcontroller Workhorse

### 2.1.1 Architecture

The **Arduino Uno** is based on the **ATmega328P** 8-bit AVR RISC microcontroller by Microchip (formerly Atmel). It uses a **Harvard architecture** with separate program and data buses.

**Core Specifications:**
- **MCU:** ATmega328P (8-bit, RISC)
- **Clock Speed:** **16 MHz** (from ceramic resonator)
- **Flash Memory (Program):** **32 KB** (0.5 KB used by bootloader)
- **SRAM (Runtime Data):** **2 KB**
- **EEPROM (Non-volatile Data):** **1 KB**
- **Operating Voltage:** **5 V**
- **Digital I/O Pins:** 14 (6 PWM)
- **Analog Input Pins:** 6 (10-bit ADC)
- **Communication:** UART, I²C, SPI

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The 6 analog pins share multiplexed ADC channels and the **ADC is 10-bit**, meaning each analog reading is quantized into $2^{10} = 1024$ discrete levels (0 to 1023).

### 2.1.2 The ADC Resolution Formula (Critical for Numerical Problems)

When the supply/reference voltage is $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$ and ADC is $n$-bit, the **step size (resolution)** is:

$$
\text{Step Size} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1}
$$

For Arduino Uno ($n = 10$, $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$):

$$
\text{Step Size} = \frac{5}{2^{10} - 1} = \frac{5}{1023} \approx 4.89\,\text{mV}
$$

This means a voltage change smaller than **~4.89 mV cannot be detected** by the Arduino Uno's ADC.

## 2.2 ESP32 — The IoT Powerhouse

### 2.2.1 Architecture

The **ESP32** is a low-cost, low-power **System-on-Chip (SoC)** manufactured by **Espressif Systems**. It integrates a **dual-core Tensilica Xtensa LX6 microprocessor** with built-in wireless connectivity.

**Core Specifications:**
- **MCU:** Dual-core Xtensa LX6 (32-bit)
- **Clock Speed:** **Up to 240 MHz**
- **SRAM:** **520 KB**
- **Flash:** **4 MB** (external SPI flash typical)
- **Operating Voltage:** **3.3 V**
- **Wireless:** **Wi-Fi 802.11 b/g/n + Bluetooth v4.2 BR/EDR + BLE**
- **GPIO Pins:** 34 (many multiplexed)
- **ADC:** Two 12-bit SAR ADCs (up to 18 channels)
- **Special:** Hall sensor, touch sensors, crypto accelerator, RTC

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** ESP32's 12-bit ADC gives a finer resolution than Arduino's 10-bit, but the ADC is non-linear and requires calibration. The onboard Wi-Fi/BT radio is the **defining feature** that makes ESP32 the de-facto IoT board.

### 2.2.2 ESP32 ADC Resolution

For ESP32 ($n = 12$, $V_{ref} \approx 3.3\,\text{V}$):

$$
\text{Step Size} = \frac{3.3}{2^{12} - 1} = \frac{3.3}{4095} \approx 0.806\,\text{mV}
$$

This is approximately **6× finer resolution** than Arduino Uno.

## 2.3 Raspberry Pi — The Single Board Computer

### 2.3.1 Architecture

The **Raspberry Pi 4 Model B** uses a **Broadcom BCM2711** SoC featuring a **quad-core ARM Cortex-A72 (64-bit)** processor.

**Core Specifications:**
- **SoC:** Broadcom BCM2711
- **CPU:** Quad-core ARM Cortex-A72 @ **1.8 GHz** (64-bit)
- **RAM:** 2 / 4 / 8 GB LPDDR4
- **Storage:** microSD card (OS) + USB 3.0
- **OS:** Raspberry Pi OS (Linux-based, Debian)
- **GPU:** VideoCore VI (OpenGL ES 3.0, 4K video)
- **Connectivity:** Wi-Fi, Bluetooth 5.0, Gigabit Ethernet
- **GPIO:** **40-pin header** (compatible with HATs)
- **Display:** 2 × micro-HDMI (4K @ 60 Hz)
- **USB:** 2 × USB 3.0 + 2 × USB 2.0

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Raspberry Pi is **NOT a microcontroller** — it is a full **Single Board Computer (SBC)**. It runs a full multitasking OS, can execute Python scripts, host web servers, run Docker, and even perform edge ML inference using TensorFlow Lite.

## 2.4 The Great Comparison — KTU Cheat Sheet

| Specification | Arduino Uno | ESP32 | Raspberry Pi 4 |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Microcontroller Board | Microcontroller SoC | Single Board Computer |
| **Core** | ATmega328P (8-bit) | Xtensa LX6 Dual (32-bit) | ARM Cortex-A72 Quad (64-bit) |
| **Clock** | **16 MHz** | **240 MHz** | **1.8 GHz** |
| **RAM** | 2 KB | 520 KB | 2–8 GB |
| **Flash** | 32 KB | 4 MB (ext.) | microSD (16 GB+) |
| **OS** | None | None / FreeRTOS | Linux (Raspbian) |
| **Wi-Fi/BT** | ❌ None | ✅ Built-in | ✅ Built-in |
| **ADC** | 10-bit | 12-bit | No native ADC (external required) |
| **Logic Level** | 5 V | 3.3 V | 3.3 V |
| **Power** | ~50 mA @ 5 V | ~80–240 mA @ 3.3 V | ~600–1500 mA @ 5 V |
| **Best For** | Real-time control, learning | Wireless IoT nodes | Edge computing, ML, gateway |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where Each Board Shines

- **Arduino Uno**: Industrial relay control, line-follower robots, academic lab exercises, deterministic motor control, low-cost sensor nodes without networking needs.
- **ESP32**: Battery-powered wireless sensor nodes, smart agriculture, wearable health monitors, MQTT-based home automation, BLE beacons, Wi-Fi mesh networks (ESP-MESH).
- **Raspberry Pi**: IoT gateway aggregating hundreds of sensor nodes, running Node-RED dashboards, hosting MQTT brokers (Mosquitto), computer vision with OpenCV, edge AI with Coral TPU, NAS, retro-gaming, Home Assistant hub.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Trick Question to Watch For:**
> *"Which board would you choose to monitor 50 temperature sensors in a factory and push data to AWS IoT Core every 10 seconds?"*
> **Answer:** An **ESP32 cluster** (for wireless + low-power nodes) reporting to a **Raspberry Pi gateway** (for data aggregation, local storage, and cloud upload). Do NOT pick Arduino Uno — it has no wireless capability.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ADC Resolution Comparison Between Boards

### 3.1.1 General Formula

An Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC) divides the reference voltage range into a finite number of discrete levels. The number of levels is determined by the number of bits $n$:

$$
L = 2^{n}
$$

The **resolution (step size)** is the voltage difference between two adjacent digital codes:

$$
\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{L - 1} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1}
$$

### 3.1.2 Arduino Uno Calculation

For Arduino Uno: $n = 10$, $V_{ref} = 5\,\text{V}$.

**Step 1:** Calculate the total number of discrete levels.

$$
L_{\text{Arduino}} = 2^{10} = 1024
$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the resolution.

$$
\Delta V_{\text{Arduino}} = \frac{5}{1024 - 1} = \frac{5}{1023}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to millivolts for readability.

$$
\Delta V_{\text{Arduino}} \approx 0.004887\,\text{V} \approx 4.887\,\text{mV}
$$

### 3.1.3 ESP32 Calculation

For ESP32: $n = 12$, $V_{ref} \approx 3.3\,\text{V}$.

**Step 1:** Total discrete levels.

$$
L_{\text{ESP32}} = 2^{12} = 4096
$$

**Step 2:** Resolution.

$$
\Delta V_{\text{ESP32}} = \frac{3.3}{4096 - 1} = \frac{3.3}{4095}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to millivolts.

$$
\Delta V_{\text{ESP32}} \approx 0.000806\,\text{V} \approx 0.806\,\text{mV}
$$

### 3.1.4 Improvement Ratio

The ratio of resolutions quantifies how much finer the ESP32 ADC is:

$$
R = \frac{\Delta V_{\text{Arduino}}}{\Delta V_{\text{ESP32}}} = \frac{4.887}{0.806} \approx 6.06
$$

> **Interpretation:** The ESP32 can detect a voltage change **~6 times smaller** than the Arduino Uno — critical for precision instrumentation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Clock Period and Instruction Cycle

### 3.2.1 Fundamental Relationship

The clock period $T$ is the reciprocal of the clock frequency $f$:

$$
T = \frac{1}{f}
$$

Most AVR microcontrollers execute **one instruction per clock cycle** (1 CPI — Clock Per Instruction), meaning:

$$
T_{\text{inst}} = T = \frac{1}{f}
$$

### 3.2.2 Arduino Uno Instruction Time

For $f_{\text{Arduino}} = 16\,\text{MHz} = 16 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Hz}$:

$$
T_{\text{Arduino}} = \frac{1}{16 \times 10^{6}} = 6.25 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{s}
$$

$$
T_{\text{Arduino}} = 62.5\,\text{ns}
$$

### 3.2.3 ESP32 Instruction Time (at 240 MHz)

$$
T_{\text{ESP32}} = \frac{1}{240 \times 10^{6}} \approx 4.167 \times 10^{-9}\,\text{s}
$$

$$
T_{\text{ESP32}} \approx 4.167\,\text{ns}
$$

### 3.2.4 Raspberry Pi 4 Instruction Time (at 1.8 GHz)

$$
T_{\text{RPi}} = \frac{1}{1.8 \times 10^{9}} \approx 5.556 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{s}
$$

$$
T_{\text{RPi}} \approx 0.556\,\text{ns}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality Check:** Although the clock speeds differ, **instructions per second (IPS) ≠ clock speed × 1**, because each architecture (AVR 1-CPI, Xtensa ~4-CPI, ARM Cortex-A72 ~1.2–1.5 CPI) has different CPI. So actual IPS is roughly proportional to $\frac{f}{\text{CPI}}$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Power Consumption Estimation

### 3.3.1 Electrical Power Formula

For a DC device, power is the product of supply voltage and current draw:

$$
P = V \times I
$$

### 3.3.2 Arduino Uno Power

For Arduino Uno: $V = 5\,\text{V}$, typical $I \approx 50\,\text{mA} = 0.05\,\text{A}$.

$$
P_{\text{Arduino}} = 5 \times 0.05 = 0.25\,\text{W} = 250\,\text{mW}
$$

### 3.3.3 ESP32 Power (Active Wi-Fi Transmission)

For ESP32 during active Wi-Fi TX: $V = 3.3\,\text{V}$, peak $I \approx 240\,\text{mA} = 0.24\,\text{A}$.

$$
P_{\text{ESP32, peak}} = 3.3 \times 0.24 = 0.792\,\text{W} \approx 792\,\text{mW}
$$

For ESP32 in **deep sleep mode**: $I \approx 10\,\mu\text{A}$.

$$
P_{\text{ESP32, sleep}} = 3.3 \times 10 \times 10^{-6} = 3.3 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{W} = 33\,\mu\text{W}
$$

This is why **ESP32 is preferred for battery-powered IoT nodes** — it spends most of its life in deep sleep, waking up briefly to transmit.

### 3.3.4 Raspberry Pi 4 Power

For Raspberry Pi 4 under load: $V = 5\,\text{V}$, $I \approx 1.2\,\text{A}$.

$$
P_{\text{RPi}} = 5 \times 1.2 = 6.0\,\text{W}
$$

This is **~24× more than Arduino Uno** and **~180,000× more than ESP32 deep sleep** — clearly not suitable for battery-only operation.

## 3.4 Derivation: Memory Footprint Comparison (Bits to Bytes)

The relationship between bits and bytes is:

$$
1\,\text{Byte} = 8\,\text{bits}
$$

### 3.4.1 Arduino Uno Flash Memory in Bytes

$$
32\,\text{KB} = 32 \times 1024\,\text{bytes} = 32{,}768\,\text{bytes} = 262{,}144\,\text{bits}
$$

### 3.4.2 ESP32 Flash Memory in Bytes

$$
4\,\text{MB} = 4 \times 1024 \times 1024 = 4{,}194{,}304\,\text{bytes} = 33{,}554{,}432\,\text{bits}
$$

### 3.4.3 Raspberry Pi 4 RAM in Bytes (4 GB variant)

$$
4\,\text{GB} = 4 \times 1024^{3} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296\,\text{bytes} = 34{,}359{,}738{,}368\,\text{bits}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** The memory hierarchy is **Flash (long-term storage) → SRAM (runtime stack/heap) → CPU registers**. Microcontrollers often have insufficient RAM for large buffers, which is why SBCs are used as gateways to aggregate data.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Selection Decision Tree

Use this matrix to choose the right board for an IoT scenario (typical KTU 14-mark question):

| Requirement | Best Choice | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Real-time motor control with sub-millisecond latency | **Arduino Uno** | Deterministic, no OS jitter, robust ATmega |
| Battery-powered temperature node with Wi-Fi (runs 1+ year) | **ESP32 (Deep Sleep)** | ~10 µA sleep current, on-chip Wi-Fi |
| Local video stream processing + MQTT broker | **Raspberry Pi 4** | Multi-core CPU, 4 GB RAM, full Linux |
| Edge AI inference (TensorFlow Lite) at sensor node | **Raspberry Pi + Coral USB** | Computational headroom for NN inference |
| BLE wearable fitness band | **ESP32** | Dual-mode BT, low power, small size |
| Interfacing 5 V industrial sensor with 3.3 V ESP32 | **Arduino Uno as translator** or use a **level shifter** | Logic-level compatibility |
| 40 sensor nodes in farm with cloud dashboard | **ESP32 nodes + Pi gateway** | Distributed architecture |

## 3.6 Baud Rate vs. Symbol Rate (For UART/Wi-Fi Questions)

The **Baud rate** $B$ (symbols per second) and **bit rate** $R_b$ are related by:

$$
R_b = B \times \log_2(M)
$$

where $M$ is the number of discrete signal levels per symbol. For binary UART (M = 2):

$$
R_b = B \times 1 = B
$$

So a **9600 baud UART transmits 9600 bits per second**, taking:

$$
T_{\text{bit}} = \frac{1}{9600} \approx 104.17\,\mu\text{s per bit}
$$

> **Application:** A 1 KB firmware image requires:

$$
T_{\text{transfer}} = \frac{8 \times 1024}{9600} = \frac{8192}{9600} \approx 0.853\,\text{s}
$$

Wi-Fi, by contrast, operates at **tens to hundreds of Mbps**, making firmware OTA updates orders of magnitude faster.
